Hi Herbert. This functionality started out in a single package called `jaraco.utils`, but there was a couple of problems with that:

1. The package was a hodgepodge of various functionality, meaning the namesake of the package told very little about what it does, which makes it obscure.
2. Each incremental change to one aspect of the package would cause a release of all aspects of the package, so anything depending on a different aspect would see flux, possibly even have to consider backward-incompatible releases that did not affect it.
3. Sometimes a backward incompatible change would occur and then a new feature would be added in a different aspect, so a user would be required to adopt the backward incompatible change in one aspect to elect the new feature in another aspect.

Honestly, I'd love for all of this functionality to be in a single package. It would simplify the management greatly. However, I'm also trying to follow the common convention that each package should represent a class of functionality or purpose.

A couple of alternative approaches that would help -

- Reduce the dependencies between packages. I suspect some of these packages aren't actually required by irc, but only by other functions in the library on which irc depends.
- Move the functionality on which irc relies to another package. I've [tried doing that with more_itertools ([here](https://github.com/erikrose/more-itertools/pull/34) and [here](https://github.com/erikrose/more-itertools/pull/37), but I gave up waiting. I also [tried contributing compose to the stdlib](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-dev/2009-August/091161.html), but even that small functionality was too controversial to reach a consensus of acceptance. So while I'm open moving this functionality to new homes, I've conceded that I'd rather just maintain the functions where I can manage them.

If you're willing to help with those activities and see them through, I'd be open to the help.

So the short answer is it's not necessary, but it's preferable for these to be in separately maintained. If you can demonstrate another widely accepted technique for solving this issue, I'm open to suggestions.

Thanks.

The exception is raised when running `gethostbyname`, which occurs *before* it tries to serve on `0.0.0.0`.
From [python 2 documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/socket.html#socket.gethostname) (emphasis mine):
> socket.gethostname()
Return a string containing the hostname of the machine where the Python interpreter is currently executing.
If you want to know the current machine’s IP address, you may want to use gethostbyname(gethostname()). **This operation assumes that there is a valid address-to-host mapping for the host, and the assumption does not always hold.**


Like @Skince said, this is probably related to `/etc/hosts`. If you look at the output of 
```
python -c "import socket; print(socket.gethostname())"
```
is it (the hostname) found in the file `/etc/hosts`? I am guessing it's not. In any case, `gdbgui` should not assume it is, so I will put a fix in to avoid this.

Haven't really thought about it yet. It is in the keras augmenter, but aside from that I've never seen it referenced in any paper and can't really find many example images regarding its effects. I guess the best way would be to just implement it and look at its effects and performance and then keep it if both factors seem good enough.
And yes, gaussian blurring should be a low pass filter as it removes high frequency components.

That is currently not easily possible. You would have to manually draw samples from the parameters inside the augmenters, e.g. something like `affine.rotate.draw_samples((number_of_images,))`. Then later on you could reinstantiate the augmenters using fixed (deterministic) values for the parameters, e.g. `Affine(rotate=37`)`. But that will be quite some work and for some augmenters it will be unclear what exactly the parameters are. E.g. for gaussian noise, do save the sampled sigma value (one float value) or the whole sampled gaussian noise map (same size as the input image)? (The latter one is also generated per image on the spot, so not easily accessible.)

What's you use case for wanting this? It seems to me like you are not going to achieve more than by just using stochastic parameters directly, as saving and loading lots of sampled values from a probability distribution is effectively the same as sampling from it directly.

The library is currently mainly aimed at uint8 images. Some augmenters probably work with other datatypes, but there isn't a thorough list for these. I would have to go through each augmenter individually to see if it works and whether it is possible to remove clip and astype commands (or, more likely, to use these only if the input dtype was uint8). For some augmenters it will definitely not be possible (e.g. Scale and Grayscale).

One Note:  Each timeout_after() is guaranteed to generate one TaskTimeout--even if nested and even if the timeouts occur at the same time.   It's possible that both the inner and outer timeout would expire at the same time in this code.  However, it would generate two exceptions.  The first would cause a cycle back to the next URL.  The second timeout would occur almost instantly after that and cause the except block to bail out to the outer function (since the expired flag would now be False).

My main project right now is a simulation of optical transmission system.

The main optics calculations are:
1. sum of two or more optical signal powers, normally done in mW. Ptotal = P1(mW) + P2(mW).
2. gain or loss of the optical channel, normally done in dBm: Pout = Pin(dBm) + gain (dB).
3. integration of optical power inside a channel, normally done in mW and finally converted to dBm.

It would be good that this could be handled automatically.

an `inspect.getfullargspec()` equivalent that works on both Python 2 and 3 would be a good addition to six.

`getfullargspec()` on Python 3 returns a named tuple with different names even though the tuple indices are the same.

`getfullargspec()` *must* be used on Python 3 if you might ever call it on a callable with annotations or keyword only arguments at times but you still want to work when running under Python 2.

I suggest just adding six.getfullargspec() that returns a Python 3 like FullArgSpec named tuple. The keyword only arguments, keyword only defaults, and annotations attributes would just be empty on Python 2.

Here's what I'm doing now in my app.

```python
class Zendesk(zdesk.Zendesk):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.thread_local = threading.local()
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    # In the light of https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/1871,
    # it's safer to initialize a fresh requests.Session in each thread.

    @property
    def client(self):
        try:
            return self.thread_local.client
        except AttributeError:
            self.thread_local.client = requests.Session()
            self._update_auth()
            return self.thread_local.client

    # This is required only because super().__init__ assigns to `self.client`.
    # If this change gets merged upstream, the assignment in __init__ and
    # this setter should be removed.
    @client.setter
    def client(self, value):
        self.thread_local.client = value
```

and the corresponding test:

```python
def test_thread_safety():
    zendesk = client.Zendesk(domain=None, email=None, token=None)
    client_in_main_thread = zendesk.client

    client_in_other_thread = None

    def save_client_in_other_thread():
        nonlocal client_in_other_thread
        client_in_other_thread = zendesk.client

    thread = threading.Thread(target=save_client_in_other_thread)
    thread.start()
    thread.join()

    assert client_in_other_thread is not client_in_main_thread
```

This proposal still creates a regression in functionality: if you change authentication information, the settings will only reflect it in the current thread's `client`. To preserve the current behavior, I think you have to call `_update_auth` at the beginning of `call`.

I think it largely depends on what software you want to be able to read the p12 files. For instance, many of the good algorithm choices for key derivation (PBKDF2) and encryption (AES 128) are only specified in relatively new versions of the PKCS12 spec, and many software platforms don't support them.

The algorithms supported by pretty much any software for reading p12s often are so weak they add a false sense of security.

So I think the complexity here is going to be determining what the different algorithm profiles for p12 files should be made available and documenting what they work with. Technically all of the pieces are available here.

- Efficiency: It should have a reasonably good (optimal or nearly optimal) efficiency at the assumed model.
- Stability: It should be robust in the sense that small deviations from the model assumptions should impair the performance only slightly, that is, the latter(described, say, in terms of the asymptotic variance of an estimate, or of the level and power of a test) should be close to the nominal value calculated at the model.
- Breakdown: Somewhat larger deviations from the model should not cause a catastrophe.

This project is a [backport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Backporting) of the [ipaddress package in the Python standard library](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ipaddress.html), aimed at Python 2.6, Python 2.7, and Python 3.2. Basically, I took the code from the Python 3.3+ ipaddress package and adapted it to older versions of Python.

If you have Python 3.3 or newer (for instance, Python 3.6), ipaddress is already available in the standard library and there is no point in installing this backport.

I don't actively break support for 3.6 and the like, but the version in the standard library will work at least as good as this backport. By the way, `3.3+` means 3.3 or any newer version, e.g. 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, 4.0.

If you're using a non-systemd Linux distribution, you may need to edit [`/etc/security/limits.conf`](https://easyengine.io/tutorials/linux/increase-open-files-limit/).

I was pushing ~16FPS with a 128x128x1 black & white OLED (sh1106) after modifications to the library, so a framerate of 3.1 on a display needing 4x the data would seem about in line with my experience.

The reason for this is that the underlying i2c transport splits the data into 32byte chunks in order to get around the buffer limitations imposed by smbus/smbus2 etc, which implement the SMBus protocol to the letter. i2c itself does not have a 32byte limit.

This is 256 chunks for 128x128x4 bits of data, resulting in 512 additional bytes (address/register) needing to be sent every single frame. Okay it's not *quite* this bad since you probably have to set a page address register every X bytes anyway, but this doesn't help.

But this is not actually necessary- smbus2 supplies an `i2c_rdwr` method which can read/write in unlimited size blocks.

It seems both `command` and `data` could depend upon a test for this method against the supplied library, and make use of it where available. Since this feature would greatly benefit the users of our 128x128 OLED breakout, I'd be happy to prepare a PR for it. I've had it running before during some of my internal testing.

Still 128x128x4 = 65536 bits, at 400kHz is 0.16384sec ignoring any address/register data or paging that might take place to transfer this kind of buffer. At 400kHz I'd say your *best* possible framerate is maybe 5-6fps so you're not really far off. Don't hold your breath for a miracle.

I'll no doubt do a lousy job explaining this, but maybe this will help...

`nflgame.players` is a dictionary with a player's `playerid` as keys and `nflgame.player.Player` class objects as values.  A `nflgame.player.Player` object has many attributes.

You can access `nflgame.players` outside of any particular game context, but the information from it is really just metadata - kind of trivial stuff like where they went to college and how many years they have played professionally.

```python
import nflgame
all_players = nflgame.players
for player_id, player in all_players.items():
    print player_id, player.name, player.college, player.years_pro
```
```
00-0027842 Stevie Brown Michigan 6
00-0027843 Austin Spitler Ohio State 4
00-0027840 Robert McClain Connecticut 5
00-0027841 Zac Robinson Oklahoma State 3
00-0023461 Chris Spencer Mississippi 11
```

Other `nflgame.player.XXX` objects can access those same attributes by calling `.player.attribute` on the object.

```python
import nflgame
games = nflgame.games(year=2016, week=7, kind='REG')
game_players = nflgame.combine_max_stats(games)
for pp in game_players:
    print pp, pp.player.college, pp.stats
```

vs

On looking at [keras/engine/training.py#L1483](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/blob/c07d0e6448bb63762bc7a19d87814f6fba79fa32/keras/engine/training.py#L1483), `'batch_size'` is not included in `params` when it is built by `fit_generator()`
```
        callbacks.set_params({
            'nb_epoch': nb_epoch,
            'nb_sample': samples_per_epoch,
            'verbose': verbose,
            'do_validation': do_validation,
            'metrics': callback_metrics,
        })
```

On the other hand, `_fit_loop()` which is called by `fit()` ([keras/engine/training.py#L855](https://github.com/fchollet/keras/blob/c07d0e6448bb63762bc7a19d87814f6fba79fa32/keras/engine/training.py#L855)) does define the `'batch_size'`.
```
        callbacks.set_params({
            'batch_size': batch_size,
            'nb_epoch': nb_epoch,
            'nb_sample': nb_train_sample,
            'verbose': verbose,
            'do_validation': do_validation,
            'metrics': callback_metrics or [],
        })
```

It turns out that `fit_generator()` does not know the batch size as it is determined by however many samples are enqueued by the `generator`. To fix this, we can count samples instead of batches. The Callback [documentation](https://keras.io/callbacks/#callback) says that number of samples in the current batch is passed as `logs['size']`. I have written a fix in [my fork](https://github.com/rohitrawat/keras-tqdm/commit/072802d0931479ccc46eb2ee0a3f5ce11b6ea347).

First thanks for taking the initiative and opening this PR. Before we can consider merging I think we will need to work on this interface a bit further. 

I won't merge something that relies on a `.pyd` file for one particular version of python and windows compiler. For long term maintainability and to be useful to the most people it is important we consider what is the best method for wrapping the vector can dll - and not just take the easiest method. I'll admit my knowledge here is very linux focused, but I'd be looking at the `ctypes` and `ffi` libraries.

As you can see on [travis](https://travis-ci.org/hardbyte/python-can) we want python-can to be compatible with Python 2.7, 3.4+, and pypy. Ideally for our Windows users we would support 7, 8, 10 in both 32 bit and 64 bit - although I'm not sure if this is the current case for all Windows interfaces. This vectorcan interface would have to also work across most - if not all - of those.

All new interfaces should have decent documentation added to the `/docs` directory including any custom installation requirements. Ideally each new interface would also have some unit tests, even though most of them won't be able to be run by our free CI.

To summarise from #73:


> @thomaspurchas
> Rather than returning True/False I think the callback should raise an exception on failure. The exception could be different depending on what went wrong.
> 
> We can then map the exceptions to the HAP protocol status values.
> 
> Using True/False feels a little bit like err values in C.

> @cdce8p
> I would think that True / False should be enough for our proposes, but you are right in that exceptions would allow for more options. @ikalchev What do you think?

> @thomaspurchas
> I would point out that True/False is not backwards compatible, and requires that users remember to return a True value on success (forgetting to return a value would result in None). This does not strike me as very pythonic.

> @ikalchev
> I just looked at Apple's spec - there are 11 error codes (which I need to add btw). If we use exceptions, we will force the Driver from understanding these exceptions and mapping them to error codes - in this case, why not set_value to return one of the error codes instead? What is more, returning an error code would allow "internal" errors to more "voicefully" propagate with an exception.

> @thomaspurchas
> There might be 11 error codes, but only some would be valid for a callback i.e. Request denied due to insufficient privileges. Unable to communicate with requested service, e.g. the power to the accessory was turned off. Resource is busy, try again. Accessory received an invalid value in a write request.
> 
> The rest are errors that should be managed by py-HAP. i.e. Operation timed out. could be thrown by setting a timeout on the callback (either via threads or asyncio).
> 
> Additionally exceptions can be nested so the following would work:
>
> ```python
> class AccessoryError(Exception):
>   pass
> 
> class AccessoryBusyError(AccessoryError):
>   pass
> 
> try:
>   throw AccessoryBusyError
> except AccessoryError as e:
>   print(e)
> ```

> @ikalchev
> True, but let's keep in mind that tomorrow Apple may add 10 more, 4 of which would make sense (for example).

> @thomaspurchas
> @ikalchev still doesn't feel pythonic. We would then need a bunch of static vars somewhere to match the docs, or rely on users parsing the docs.

Jonathan,

I've been running some experiments in hopes of writing a filtering cookbook page for the Eliot documentation.

Based on my observations (sample code below) I think the following statements are true.  Can you confirm?

1. Using ``--select`` to filter out events (with a ``!=`` expression) does not guarantee that tasks will be rendered in order.  [ See Case 1 and Case 5 in the example below; I find that the results are un-deterministic.  Sometimes ``second_action`` is rendered before ``first_action`` ]
2. The help says "If any child task is selected the _entire_ top-level task is selected."  In practice what I see is that A) the top level task's uuid is rendered B) the selected event(s) within that task are rendered. [ See Case 2 below.  A single event is selected, and only that event and the associated top level task's uuid are rendered ]
3. The help says that ``--select``  "...can be specified multiple times to mimic logical AND.".  Since JMESPath supports the ``&&`` operator,  I find that ANDs can be implemented in a single ``--select`` [ See Case 4 and 5 below ].  I couldn't find a way to get multiple ``--select`` parameters to work [ See cases 'Multi Select' 0 through 2 below; passing multiple selects neither seemed to behave like OR nor like AND]

Thanks!

```Python
from eliot import start_action, to_file, Message
import subprocess

to_file(open("example.log", "w"))
with start_action(action_type="first_action"):
    Message.log(message_type="some_message", level='in_test', data=list(range(3)))
    Message.log(message_type="some_message", level='in_production', data='spam')

try:
    with start_action(action_type="second_action"):
        Message.log(message_type="some_message", level='in_test', data=list(range(3, 5)))
        Message.log(message_type="some_message", level='in_production', data='eggs')
        raise RuntimeError("Broken pram")
except:
    pass

select_expressions = [
    None,
    '"level != \'in_test\'"',
    '"action_status == \'failed\'"',
    '"task_level[0] == \\`3\\`"',
    '"level == \'in_test\' && data[0] == \\`3\\`"',
    '"level != \'in_test\' && level != \'in_production\'"',
    ]

print('')
for case, select_expression in enumerate(select_expressions):
    command = 'eliot-tree'
    if select_expression:
        command += ' --select ' + select_expression 
    command += ' example.log'
    print('Case: {}'.format(case))
    print(command)
    print('=' * len(command))
    subprocess.call(command, shell=True)

select_expressions = [
    ('"level != \'in_test\'"', '"level != \'in_production\'"'),
    ('"level == \'in_test\'"', '"level == \'in_production\'"'),
    ('"level == \'in_test\'"', '"level == \'in_test\'"'),
    ]
    
for case, (select_expression1, select_expression2) in enumerate(select_expressions):

    command = 'eliot-tree --select {} --select {} example.log'.format(select_expression1, select_expression2)
    print(command)
    print('=' * len(command))
    subprocess.call(command, shell=True)

Have a look at this *data replaying* sample for your 5 minute data. It filters all data happening before 06:35 and any data happening after 17:30.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8; py-indent-offset:4 -*-
###############################################################################
from __future__ import (absolute_import, division, print_function,)
#                        unicode_literals)

import datetime

import backtrader as bt
import backtrader.feeds as btfeeds
import backtrader.filters as btfilters
import backtrader.indicators as btind


class St(bt.Strategy):
    def __init__(self):
        atrdata = self.data1 if len(self.datas) > 1 else self.data0
        self.atr = btind.ATR(atrdata, period=5, movav=btind.MovAv.SMA)

    def next(self):
        dt00 = self.data0.datetime.datetime().isoformat()
        dt10 = self.data1.datetime.date().isoformat()
        print('%d: Minute %s Date %s %.4f' %
              (len(self), dt00, dt10, self.atr[0]))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    cerebro = bt.Cerebro()
    cerebro.addstrategy(St)

    data0 = btfeeds.GenericCSVData(
        dataname='tsla-5min.csv',
        timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Minutes,
        compression=5,
        sessionstart=datetime.time(6, 31),
        sessionend=datetime.time(17, 30),
    )

    data0.addfilter(btfilters.SessionFilter)
    cerebro.adddata(data0)

    cerebro.replaydata(data0, timeframe=bt.TimeFrame.Days)

    cerebro.run()
```

Ah, I think I understand your confusion here.  The MiniJsonExtractor parses JSON before doing extraction, so if you're extracting a full data structure (a list or dictionary), it returns a python object, not a text string.  When used in a template, this may give unexpected results.

There's a couple solutions to this problem:

* **Simplest:** just extract simple fields (not full structures) and use them in a template like so:
https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/content-test.yaml#L46

*A little clunkier I know, but usable.*

* **More powerful, but more complex:**  define a custom extractor (possible extending the JsonPathMini extractor) that converts the python object back to JSON strings.   See [extensions documentation](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/extensions.md).  It's perfectly safe to go in core if it doesn't add additional libraries, but does need unit testing. Would suggest extractor named jsonpath_mini_string.  **Another option would be a regex-based extractor (also valid, maybe more broadly useful).**  If you have the time, this would make a wonderful PR since it shouldn't be more than a couple hours work (otherwise I can add it as a future enhancement).

* **More powerful but harder option** define a custom extractor extension using [jsonpath-rw](https://github.com/kennknowles/python-jsonpath-rw) that offers even more power (on my radar as a future enhancement already).  This may be set to autoload on start if the library is installed, much like the [json schema validator](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/pyresttest/ext/validator_jsonschema.py). 

* **Superman mode:** add Jinja2 templating support as a optional extension (same mode as above). This one is a future goal after the next [major set of planned changes](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/46) unless I can find a clever and back-compatible way to insert it.  It will take a long time before it's an option though (read: probably months). 

Does that both answer your question and give some idea of where future options are going?

This is expected behaviour, as currently the algorithm only considers supports of equal size <http://nashpy.readthedocs.io/en/stable/reference/support-enumeration.html> which in turn only implies it will find equilibria for non degenerate games. In this case your game is degenerate as, for example, the first row has two best responses (the last two columns).

I have this issue up https://github.com/drvinceknight/Nashpy/issues/40 which I might get around to at some point (the idea being to add a flag that allows for the consideration of unequal sized support sets) and you can also read more about this at #39 where I also briefly discuss testing for degeneracy.

@ecdsa I don't think I want to implement pruning.  I suspect the vast majority of transactions and DB space is occupied by addresses with minimal re-use, and that this will only become more true going forwards.

Each little-used address is more expensive for the server: it requires a key and small value in the history DB, whereas a heavily-used address requires only a handful of keys and uses many larger values.

I think the reason electrum-server implemented pruning was that it stored all the history in a single value, and the read-append-write cycle was inefficient.  ElectrumX doesn't have this issue.

Also I have plans in the not-too-distant future to improve ElectrumXs history handling by fixing #348 and #185.

So I see this as ultimately a non-issue - the cost of supporting big histories will be small compared to the cost of supporting histories for all small addresses.  Please re-open if you disagree.

@ponsperello90, what you have there should work:

```
>>> s = v.All(
...   v.Schema({v.Required(v.Any('a', 'b')): v.Any({"a1": int}, {"b1": int})}),
...   v.Schema({'a': {"a1": int}, 'b': {"b1": int}}))
>>> s({})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: required key not provided @ data[Any(['a', 'b'])]
>>> s({'a': 'a'})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected a dictionary for dictionary value @ data['a']
>>> s({'a': {'b1': 2}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data['a']['b1']
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 'a2'}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected int for dictionary value @ data['a']['a1']
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 1}})
{'a': {'a1': 1}}
>>> s({'b': 'b'})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected a dictionary for dictionary value @ data['b']
>>> s({'b': {'a1': 1}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data['b']['a1']
>>> s({'b': {'b1': 'b2'}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected int for dictionary value @ data['b']['b1']
>>> s({'b': {'b1': 2}})
{'b': {'b1': 2}}
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 1}, 'b': {'b1': 2}})
{'a': {'a1': 1}, 'b': {'b1': 2}}
```

However, it can actually be simplified if you take advantage of the fact that voluptuous makes cascaded calls (à la [`reduce()`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#reduce)) to each callable passed into `All()` from left to right. In this way, you can use the first clause to assert the correctness of they keys, and lean entirely on the second clause to assert the sanity of the values:

```
>>> s = v.All(
...   v.Schema({v.Required(v.Any('a', 'b')): object}),
...   v.Schema({'a': {'a1': int}, 'b': {'b1': int}}))
>>> s({})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: required key not provided @ data[Any(['a', 'b'])]
>>> s({'a': 'a'})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected a dictionary for dictionary value @ data['a']
>>> s({'a': {'b1': 2}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data['a']['b1']
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 'a2'}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected int for dictionary value @ data['a']['a1']
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 1}})
{'a': {'a1': 1}}
>>> s({'b': 'b'})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected a dictionary for dictionary value @ data['b']
>>> s({'b': {'a1': 1}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: extra keys not allowed @ data['b']['a1']
>>> s({'b': {'b1': 'b2'}})
voluptuous.error.MultipleInvalid: expected int for dictionary value @ data['b']['b1']
>>> s({'b': {'b1': 2}})
{'b': {'b1': 2}}
>>> s({'a': {'a1': 1}, 'b': {'b1': 2}})
{'a': {'a1': 1}, 'b': {'b1': 2}}
```

This works because the incoming object is first validated against the `{v.Required(v.Any('a', 'b')): object}` clause, which, so long as it has either `'a'` or `'b'` (or both) top-level keys, will pass; the output is then put through the second clause, which goes one step farther: it demands that the (optional) `'a'` key, if it exists, *must* contain data like `{'a1': int}`; the same goes for the `'b'` key.

Both styles should be equivalent; the latter will just be a hare faster because there will be fewer function calls involved in asserting the data.

For now I use `Fresh-Token` header in my API responses so client should not call `/auth` endpoint every time it's expired but on each response has new fresh token generated on backend.
So in this way I just set the token to expire in 3 minutes but if user is still active in the client-side application, it will receive fresh token in responses and have an experience of "continuous session".
And additionally you don't need to log-out user since the token's lifetime is too short. So if no any activity in 3 minutes, the token will be expired and the "session" will die.
And in such scenarios I would like to have some public method provided by the interface of the `JWT` instance to make able to just call `jwt.generate_token()`  and easily get fresh token, maybe using the old one which is not expired yet.

What do you think about such stuff in the public interface?

There is no vanilla way to invalidate tokens on the server side. On the client side, a /logout should simply delete the token (refer to Satellizer - https://github.com/sahat/satellizer).

To implement an invalidate on the server side, you'll need to use a short token expiration followed by a refresh token feature (https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3487991/why-does-oauth-v2-have-both-access-and-refresh-tokens and https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-jwt/issues/29)

Also, please consider using StackOverflow for some of these issues - as they're more questions about functionality, than issues (might get faster responses - and it helps the community)

Yes - the library doesn't do well with empty shapefiles.  Null shapefile types should work, but that's different than empty.  I can't think of a good use case where you'd want an empty shapefile lying around but let me know if there is one.  

Opening the shapefile in QGIS, it appears there are only 7 columns: "Id", "shortintfi", "longintfie", "floatfield", "doublefiel", "textfield", "datefield".  The "FID" you see in ArcGIS is a virtual construct created on the fly and not an actual column.  You can easily demonstrate this by deleting a feature.  As long as you  have the shapefile open in ArcMap, the FID column number will skip the missing feature.  But if you save the shapefile, remove it, and re-add it, ArcMap will renumber the FID column.

Based on the error message this is not related to an empty shapefile. Rather, it is related to the code reaching the end of file (returning fewer than the needed 32 bytes) when reading the fields in the dbf file, which would make sense if there were no records after the header. But the error is because the lib believes there are more fields than there actually is. This could be because the calculation code is wrong, but this has not been a problem until now, and I have tested that reading empty shapefiles indeed works, even with zero fields. So more likely I suspect you might have had a file where the dbfheaderlength was misreported. Closing this for now, unless you have reason to believe this is a systematic error. 

(It could also be a rare issue with the dbf deletion field not being present if that is at all possible, but that would require diving into the dbf spec).

after some thought I believe my previous proposal wasn't ideal. the class attribute value serves as default value (or function) to the field. for example the `timestamp` field default value could be `datetime(2018, 1, 1)` or the function `datetime.now`. 

it doesn't feel right to run the "default function" over the passed value, so I think the best approach to your example is:

```python
from datetime import datetime

from dateutil import parser
from simple_model import Model


class MyModel(Model):
    name: str = ''
    timestamp: datetime
    # default value is already None, no need to set it
    # I believe parser.parse always return a datetime object so it would be nice to define it

    def __post_init__(self, **kwargs):
        self.timestamp = parser.parse(self.timestamp)
        self.clean()  # I'm not the biggest fan of calling clean() on post init. but if you think it's really necessary here is the best place to do it (after the parsing)


data = {'name': 'Name', 'timestamp': '2018-02-05T10:00:01.000001Z'}
model = MyModel(**data)

dict(model)  # no exception is raised here
```

The case you provided feats the goal of the `__post_init__` method. Its objective is to give the possibility of performing further operations on the model and its fields after simple model internal initializations.

IMO this solve the issue you opened, @fvlima. Thanks for your question and if you have any others doubts or suggestions feel free to comment here or open other issues.

I prefer below logic.

1. `rpm-py-installer` tries to get the archive from `http://lists.rpm.org`.
2. if it gets an error in above process such as network error, try to get the archive from Github.

The HTTP URL http://ftp.rpm.org/releases is officially announced in the RPM upstream project.
But the HTTPS URL https://ftp.rpm.org/releases has not been announced so far.

There is a list of available filter operators in Redmine's [source code](http://www.redmine.org/projects/redmine/repository/entry/trunk/app/models/query.rb#L174). I believe you can use something like this:

```python
issues = redmine.issue.filter(project_id='PCA', subject='~foo')

I spent a few minutes futzing with this, but I think I'm inclined to leave this as it is. It's not that spaces are treated as words, rather it's that if there are leading spaces, the first word isn't special-cased, so the "small words" rules come into play, i.e.:

    $ titlecase ' to turn a tide'
     to Turn a Tide
    $ titlecase ' now turn a tide'
     Now Turn a Tide

It feels to me that leading whitespace is a bit odd to pass into this library in the first place, but if you did, it would most likely be in service of some form of partial string titlecasing, in which case you probably don't want to capitalize leading small words.

I don't feel strongly about this and would be open to arguments to change behavior if there were some good examples for when you'd titlecase strings with leading whitespace, but not strip it, and want the first characters capitalized.

Hi !

I have multiple instances working in both http and https without any problem.

I think this is related to Flask/Werkzeug and the way it handles https.

If you want https, you need to make sure your reverse-proxy is setting the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header properly.
If set, Flask/Werkzeug will set the `request.is_secure` to `True` (this is a good way to check this is correctly detected) and `url_for` will return https URLs (in the same request context).

The solution you provide should be working, but you won't be able anymore to work in http.

import time
import PySimpleGUI as sg

def CubeTimer():
    # Show a form that's a running counter
    form = sg.FlexForm('Running Timer', auto_size_text=True)
    text_element = sg.Text('', size=(10, 2), font=('Helvetica', 20), justification='center')
    form_rows = [[sg.Text('Rubix Cube Timer')],
                 [text_element],
                 [sg.T(' ' * 15), sg.ReadFormButton('Start/Stop', focus=True), sg.Quit()]]
    form.LayoutAndRead(form_rows, non_blocking=True)

    # logic for reading the form buttons, starting/stopping timer
    timer_running = True
    i = 0
    while True:
        if timer_running:
            i += 1
        text_element.Update('{:02d}:{:02d}.{:02d}'.format((i//100)//60, (i//100)%60, i%100))
        button, values = form.ReadNonBlocking()
        if values is None or button == 'Quit':      # if user closed the window using X or clicked Quit button
            break
        elif button == 'Start/Stop':
            timer_running = not timer_running

        time.sleep(.01)
        # if the loop finished then need to close the form for the user
    form.CloseNonBlockingForm()
    del(form)

CubeTimer()

Link to deployment of multiple worker processes: https://python-socketio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#multi-process-deployments.

I really see no benefit of having mid-connection WSGI environments, so far I haven't seen any compelling reason to implement this option. The different ways in which long-polling and websocket transports are implemented make the use of environments at times other than connection unreliable.

> And to update cookies from event handlers

I believe we discussed this above. You certainly understand your application much better than I do, but updating cookies from event handlers is not a good idea. You are not guaranteed that an event handler will trigger an HTTP response back to the client. I think you need to stop thinking about events in terms of HTTP requests. They are not the same, the rules are different.

But if the client authenticates itself at some point within the socketio connection, for example by triggering a login event, currently the only way to remember that he has authenticated is to store this information together with his sid somewhere. And if you are running multiple workers, you also need to synchronize them.

By having a recent environment available, you can let your framework of choice do session handling for you. And this usually works for multiple workers out of the box. Django for instance can store whatever you want in a session and make that data available in subsequent requests no matter what worker they run in.

No, [00 isn't supposed to behave the same as +](https://github.com/googlei18n/libphonenumber/blob/master/FAQ.md#why-wasnt-00-parsed-the-same-as-), it's country-specific.

That input is the byte string for the UTF-8 encoding of `u'\u200b1-123-456-789'` (where `\u200b` is [ZERO WIDTH SPACE](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/200B/index.htm)), but the library is currently expecting (on Python 2.x) to receive a valid string -- either a `unicode` string or a plain `str` string with ASCII contents.

I'll think about whether it makes sense to unilaterally assume any `str` input is actually UTF-8, but in the meanwhile you can probably work around the problem by ensuring you feed in `unicode` objects.

Actually, I never tested twitter with convert token and I just figured out it does not work exactly as I expected.
In fact the social auth backend expects a query string instead of a simple token.
You can figure this out by looking at the do_auth function of the python-social-auth backend (in this case the twitter backend code).

I managed to get this to work by putting `oauth_token=<token>&oauth_token_secret=<secret_token>` in the token field.

So in a curl command this should look like this : `curl -X POST -d "grant_type=convert_token&client_id=<client_id>&backend=twitter&token=oauth_token=<token>%26oauth_token_secret=<secret_token>" http://localhost:8000/auth/api/convert-token`
%26 is the ampersand character (&) encoded.

Well, this is not really user friendly but I don't want to put special cases everywhere in the code and I prefer to let the backend/user couple handle this. I chose the same behavior (i.e. not doing anything) for error responses as all backends won't respond the same way.

Feel free to close the issue when you are okay with this

Yeah, I've (informally) been using this approach:

- Comment: undecided / not a full review
- Approve: Good to go, or I may have pointed out a specific concern.
  - If the concern is unclear to OP, then please clarify before merging
  - If the OP comes up with a different solution to the concern, go ahead and merge
  - If the concern is clearly a misunderstanding, go ahead and merge. OP should be on the lookout for followup clarifications (like the one below the break)
  - If the OP acknowledges the concern, but thinks it's not problematic, please discuss before merging.
- Request Changes: 
  - There's a concern, but I (reviewer) haven't identified a satisfactory solution. OP should post a solution and wait for re-review
  - There's a concern, and a potential solution is suggested. There are subtleties: alternative solutions may be unacceptable. OP should post a solution and wait for re-review.

This approach provides a lot of clarity to OP about next steps: :heavy_check_mark: is completely in the hands of OP to merge at their discretion (although follow-up may be expected). A :x: always implies further discussion or review is expected.

Alright, this is an issue specifically with `ConciseContract`.

I see two routes

- Implement a `__dir__` method which returns a tuple of all of the contract function names.
- Change the `__init__` of concise contract to actually populate self with all of the contract functions using the same logic currently present in the `__getattr__`.

`StackingEstimator` in step 3 should add synthetic feature(s) to left of the features from step 2 RobustScaler.

There is not a very simply way to access those transformed features in the intermediate steps. One work-around is to build a partial pipeline to refit dataset and then to get the transformed features (see a demo below). But by using this way, please set a fix `random_state` if the operators in the pipeline (including the nested operator 'BernoulliNB') has the parameter for getting consistent results.

```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_digits
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import RobustScaler
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from tpot.builtins import StackingEstimator
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFwe, f_classif
from sklearn.naive_bayes import BernoulliNB


digits = load_digits()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(digits.data, digits.target,
                                                    train_size=0.75, test_size=0.25)


partial_pipeline = make_pipeline(
SelectFwe(score_func=f_classif, alpha=0.048),
RobustScaler(),
StackingEstimator(estimator=BernoulliNB(alpha=0.01, fit_prior=True)),
)

partial_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
# get transformed X_train after step 3
X_transformed_after_step3 = partial_pipeline.transform(X_train)
print(X_transformed_after_step3.shape)
```

If we take there being no difference between the simple and component items to the logical extreme then we end up not writing XML at all but simply the `VPrinter` output. Writing and parsing the XML is useful as it allows us to put in more information than you would get through the `SimpleItem`'s `repr`/`eval` serialisation cycle. It makes forward and backward compatibility easier since we can construct the schema attributes manually and manipulate them before giving them to the object, we can store the plugin class and schema version too.

All we need to do to fix the `ShareDir` item is to change (in e.g. `Executable`) the `is_prepared` attribute to be defined as:
```python
'is_prepared': ComponentItem('shareddirs', defvalue=None, optional=True,  load_default=False, doc='Location of shared resources...'),
```

I've just tested this and it works as far as I can tell. It works because when reading the XML, the parser bases its decision on how to read the object on the XML text, not the Ganga type so it simple `eval`s it and assigns it to `is_prepared`. Next time it writes it, it will write it as full XML as it should be.

As an aside though, why do we store the `ShareRef` on an application object at all? It stores the location of the share dir but can that not be calculated from the hash of the application? I feel like all `ShareRef` objects should only exist within the prep registry anyway and not also sometimes be attached to job objects.

Current order of events is (with ~nothing in between):

1. Write new data to data.new
2. Move old data to data~
3. Move data.new to data

i.e. data~ is a backup from the **previous** flush, not the one that is being written. I personally think this makes more sense in that if you have a corrupt flush you always have the previous one to go back to though I can see the argument for basically having data~ == data

I believe the docker container is running the base PyKMIP 0.6 (released last December), which does not have the newer certificate and ``shared_ciphers`` features. It should work out of the box with a basic set of certificates, which from the logs you provided appears to be the case. Unfortunately I am not familiar with the order of operations used by the vSphere web client, so I do not know if the repeated occurrence of ``DiscoverVersions`` is normal behavior or not. Do the log files look the same when you compare using the docker container with the patched version of master?

If you're running the master branch from Github (which you were originally), you'll see the ``shared_ciphers`` error and also hit certificate issues if your certificate does not have the extended key usage extension. You can set ``enable_tls_client_auth`` to ``False`` in the ``server.conf`` configuration file, which will turn off the strict check for the extended key usage extension. The certificates you use with the docker container should work then.

I'll merge in the ``shared_ciphers`` bug fix today and then master should work for you regardless of which Python version you're using. That should hopefully resolve all of the errors you were running into.

This could be tricky. The current semantics of `RegisterResource` are that the step is ready to execute at the moment that we see the event. In order to do this we'd need to have access to a component resource's list of child resources in the language host so that we can wait for them to resolve before invoking the engine.

More palatable to me sounds like relaxing the invariant on `RegisterResource` and allowing unready requests enter the engine. The engine could still require that all properties be resolved, but it could wait until all `dependsOn` resources resolve before scheduling the step to execute. This jives with the notion of the engine being a generalized DAG executor a little more than the former does.

This just feels like a bug, unsurprising given our history.

`pulumi destroy` and `pulumi stack rm` should not require the `Pulumi.yaml` file, because neither actually runs the program.  So, I believe we should be able to fix these.

`pulumi stack select` probably doesn't make sense to try to make work in this context, since its entire purpose is to select a stack so that subsequent commands automatically use the ambiently selected stack (which we are intentionally overriding using the `--stack` flag).

`pulumi stack ls` has an `--all` (`-a`) flag that will list all stacks, regardless of project file.

If you ignore PEP8 warnings `7497-6838 =659` things to fix.

https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8 is nice and you can configure things to ignore https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e7c1e6a8e39df0d206efe247f5eb0481eb8b8b6c/setup.cfg


maybe you can focus in what to ignore from the top of the warnings (because they show the way a programmer do things, or the more prevalent things on fast ai source code)


```
$ pep8 --statistics -qq . | sort -nr
3110    E231 missing whitespace after ','
1938    W191 indentation contains tabs
669     E501 line too long (83 > 79 characters)
619     E261 at least two spaces before inline comment
266     E302 expected 2 blank lines, found 1
188     E701 multiple statements on one line (colon)
164     E225 missing whitespace around operator
142     E128 continuation line under-indented for visual indent
130     E111 indentation is not a multiple of four
55      E301 expected 1 blank line, found 0
54      W293 blank line contains whitespace
```

I think `W191` should be fixed automatically because it contains tabs! :).

which is the largest line in the code?

So your configure could be some like

```
[pep8]
max-line-length = 512
ignore = E231

[flake8]
max-line-length = 512
```
with this you will skip 3110 `some,other` and 669 lines to loong. 3779 skips of 7497. Fixing  3718, maybe it will be nice to configure it and run it one time, so in the future the configuration could be checked.

Remember PEP8 is a guidance, but if your repo does have some specifics, they could be keep in place, you only need to configure things out, so that all people could follow and check if needed... that means that you can choose to ignore (maybe corrects the ones with less than 100 ocurrences in code, or maybe less than 50).

This is an error because its attempting to write to protected system folder in /Library/....

You have 2 options of how to install packages using pip

  1.

Use a virtual environment for the by installing virtualenv to create isolated environments for each python package you may want to use or version. The benefit here is that you do not change anything in the system that could cause problems later. https://packaging.python.org/guides/installing-using-pip-and-virtualenv/
The quick gist here is pip install --user virtualenv
then goto a new directory like mkdir ~/planetcli
go into that directory cd ~/planetcli
create a vritualenv python -m virtualenv planet
then activate it source env/bin/activate
do the same command with sudo pip install planet
profit from the planet cmd.

  1.

Use sudo to install the package. While this is easier its potential to mess with things on the system is usually not the way go because the installed versions of libraries maybe forced to change by new pip installed programs. This can cause previous tools to stop working later for example.
sudo pip install planet

Option 1 is a typical way to use pip to install packages and i would suggest it.
Hope this helps!

It occurs to me that this should also be where we declare if a stream is direct or not.  Not sure what the cleanest way to deal with that is though.

As it stands, I wanted to allow people to specify output_fields for multiple streams via a dict, like:

```python
class ComplexBolt(Bolt):
    output_fields = {'default': ['foo', 'bar'],
                     'other': ['baz']}
```

but we should also squeeze in a `bool` for direct (defaulting to `False`) somewhere.

I think the cleanest way to handle this is to add a `Stream` class that looks like this:

```py
class Stream(object):
    def __init__(self, name='default', fields=None, direct=False):
        self.name = name
        self.fields = fields if fields is not None else []
        self.direct = direct
```

and then have people declare `streams` instead of `output_fields`:

```py
class ComplexBolt(Bolt):
    streams = [Stream(fields=['foo', 'bar']), Stream(name='other', fields=['baz'])]
```

This approach would also sidestep all of the metaprogramming issues this PR currently has because `streams` wouldn't be a property anymore.  The only downside is that it's a little more verbose.

Hi @tuvistavie,

You are correct: for best performance, some preprocessing should be applied to the data (both train and test) before using fastText. As you mentioned, a common preprocessing step is tokenization. Other include lowercasing or various text normalization. As there is no "all-purpose" preprocessing (e.g. tokenization is language dependent), we decided to only use very basic tokenization and let the user preprocess the data.

As @siavash9000 mentioned, several tools exist for tokenization, such as the Stanford Tokenizer (https://nlp.stanford.edu/software/tokenizer.html). For English, a very crude preprocessing can be obtained using
```
>> cat data.txt | sed -e "s/\([\.\!?,'/()]\)/ \1 /g" | tr "[:upper:]" "[:lower:]" > data.preprocessed.txt
```

@t-b I wonder if the abstractfeatureseries might help for this?
https://nwb-schema.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html#abstractfeatureseries
The abstractfeatureseries is a time series that specifies values of stimulus features over time.  The values of the features are stored in the "data" dataset of the timeseries.
Perhaps one of the columns in the "data" dataset (values of one of the features) could be used to indicate the type of stimulus, e.g. 1 = "Short Square", 2="Ramp", ... and other features could be used to specify the parameters of the given stimulus, e.g. spatial frequency of a short square.  The "features" dataset would contain a legend specifying what the different columns in "data" contain.  If the stimulus could be completely described by an abstractfeatureseries, then in the description field for PatchClampSeries you could specify what the corresponding abstractfeatureseries stimulus was.

Async/Tasklets was the first thing I looked for when I cracked open gcloud-python. As a long-time GAE user, I've grown to love tasklets because they allow a developer to write performant async code while still allowing for proper separation of concerns / encapsulation. Meaning: to do RPC work in parallel, the developer doesn't need to jam a whole bunch of otherwise unrelated work into a single function.

That said, the NDB implementation of tasklets is definitely a big undertaking and has challenging issues like the ones that @dhermes points out above (https://code.google.com/p/googleappengine/issues/detail?id=9610).

I see that each of `gcloud.datastore`, `gcloud.storage`, and `gcloud.pubsub` (to a certain extent) at least have the notion of batching, but it appears that this batching has been implemented separately in each package meaning, e.g., I can't batch a storage request together with a datastore request (please correct me if I'm wrong).

I don't yet know enough about the API backend for the Google APIs, but does it support a more global sense of batching? E.g., can gcloud have a higher-level notion of batching that will work over all (most?) of the sub-APIs?

"Higher-level" batching isn't as powerful as full-on tasklets, but at least it would allow the performance gains, i.e., across disparate APIs.

> I don't understand the error I'm getting. It happens only under 2.7. Am I holding `functools` wrong?
> ```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/var/code/gcp/bigquery/tests/unit/test_client.py", line 297, in test_get_dataset
    dataset = client.get_dataset(dataset_ref)
  File "/var/code/gcp/bigquery/google/cloud/bigquery/client.py", line 288, in get_dataset
    api_call = retry(api_call)
  File "/var/code/gcp/.nox/unit-2-7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/api/core/retry.py", line 247, in __call__
    @six.wraps(func)
  File "/var/code/gcp/.nox/unit-2-7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/six.py", line 811, in wrapper
    f = functools.wraps(wrapped, assigned, updated)(f)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/functools.py", line 33, in update_wrapper
    setattr(wrapper, attr, getattr(wrapped, attr))
AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute '__module__'
```

When you create a function on the fly with `functools.partial`, it does not assign it a `__module__` property, which is assigned when a function is built with the `def` keyword. `@six.wraps` (which is just an alias to `functools.wraps`) requires a `__module__` property for aliasing the docstring.

You can get around it with:

```python
# Make the partial that you were making before.
partial_func = functools.partial(func, *args, **kwargs)

# Assign it an appropriate __module__ property based on the function it wraps.
partial_func.__module__ = func.__module__
```

After that it should work with `wraps`.

Isn't *quite* possible with the current implementation of scale functions in layouts. The issue appears to be those more complex conditionals in the middle that operate on more than one field.

I can also see how recursive scale functions would accommodate that, but in my experience recursion is best regarded as a last resort. It certainly has its uses, but it's probably overly complex for emulating simple conditional logic.

I'm thinking more along the lines of expanding the scale function layout directive to allow for a single field or a list of named fields to operate on. Then you could define a scale function (e.g. `effect_direction`) that expects both a beta and an OR (gracefully handling undefined values) and returns a direction (-1, 0, or 1). Then the layout directive that defines the point shape would require an array of three values:

1. `if` scale function object mapping the LD ref var boolean to the diamond shape (as currently implemented in the standard association layout)
2. `effect_direction` function object mapping the beta and OR fields to the triangles or circle
3. Just the  string "circle" in case the above two both fail

This is a pretty minor expansion to how layouts parse scalable value directives and would allow for passing arbitrarily many fields to a scale function, which already has the scaffolding to allow for custom definitions. It *looks* like this would solve your problem without requiring recursion and without pushing more logic into the data source (which, in principle, should do minimal operations on the data itself). But maybe I'm missing something?

@kootenpv `dtstart` is a fundamental part of the rule, because it serves as the basis for the recurrences. I don't recall precisely, but I wouldn't be surprised if the RFC doesn't specify a way for rules to propagate "backwards" starting at the start date. `between` returns dates matching the rule between two dates, which doesn't apply to dates before `dtstart`.

@pquentin I just meant that an immediate release is not important because the bug only happens on certain days of the month, and we happen to be in a 30-day month anyway, so it's not likely to affect much of anything before we get a chance to do a proper feature release anyway.

Note that this bug only occurs when using the default argument for the default date. If you use, e.g. `parse('2014-04', default=datetime(2015, 05, 31))`, you'll get an error no matter what day you run the command on, whereas if you use `parse('2014-04', default=datetime(2015, 05, 01))`, you'll never get an error. You can use a hybrid approach by using `parse('2014-04', default=datetime.today().replace(day=1))`, which returns the first of a given month when no day is specified.

I think this is done. In summary we have two options for the tox command:

```
- nodeenv --prebuilt {toxworkdir}/node_env
bash -c '. {toxworkdir}/node_env/bin/activate; npm install; npm run <node_script>'
```
This creates just one node environment shared by all of the tox environments and it also persists between runs so is the fastest by far. Downside here is that we need to execute a bash command to activate the node environment and run the npm commands within that environment.
```
nodeenv -p --prebuilt
npm install
npm run <node_script>
```
This uses the path of the python virtualenv and the npm commands can be executed as-is. Downside here is that the node environment is created every time for every tox environment so this is quite a slow process.

I prefer the first option since the tox runs are much quicker. But the second will probably be more portable.

Any preferences or suggestions from anyone?

I think I want to push back on the philosophy in the answer a bit, though. It is great that these are configurable values. On a related point, I have started to create coding paradigms that allow me not to lose computationally expensive results, like @betatim was worried about:

```python
if net.saved():
    net.load()
    net.plot_loss_acc()
else:
    net.train(epochs=10000, accuracy=1.0, report_rate=50, 
              tolerance=0.4, batch_size=len(net.dataset.train_inputs), 
              plot=True, record=100)
    net.save()
```
That is, the first time through, it runs (trains a neural network, can take a long time) but subsequently it reloads the network. Output is the same either way. (Can also be accomplished with variables in previous cells). This kind of strategy can be part of recommendations for best-practices.

So one thing to note is that the [copy()](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Bucket.copy) is already multithreaded as noted in the docs. How large are the files you are transferring typically?

I have a feeling what is happening is that the threads may be starving themselves in the sense that you have 20 threads making ``copy()`` calls but for each ``copy()``, it alone can make 10 concurrent requests if the files are relatively large (in terms of MB). So in a worst case scenario you can have 200 threads making requests at the same time and that point you maybe throttling yourself a lot. One thing that you may want to do if that is the case is configure the managed copy to use less threads by passing a [TransferConfig](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/customizations/s3.html#boto3.s3.transfer.TransferConfig) object to the ``copy()`` method.

Let me know if that helps.

I tested using `ThreadingMixin` and `ForkingMixin` simply inheriting them in addition to `SimpleXMLRPCServer`. Here are findings:

1. Allows server to process multiple requests simultaneously as expected. Basically this means that one long lasting keyword doesn't block using other keywords.

2. Somewhat surprisingly there were no performance benefits whatsoever when running tests with quick keywords in parallel. My guess is that the overhead of threads/processes is too big compared to benefits when keywords are run really fast.

3. The current code for intercepting stdout/stderr isn't threadsafe. Currently we just do `sys.stdout = StringIO()` and later `sys.stdout.getvalue()` and `sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__`, but that doesn't work if multiple threads are changing `sys.stdout`. Results included `AttributeError` when using `sys.stdout.getvalue()` after another thread had already restored `sys.stdout` and also segfault with error `Fatal Python error: GC object already tracked`. We somehow needed to have own `sys.stdout` for each thread.

I'm not sure the rounding mode you're using in the query matches what you wrote in your text.

If you want to always round away from zero in this situation then you could use `roundTowardNegative`.

I agree with your conventions, especially to use longer "talking" names. Here are my thoughts on some of the the bold ones (I have no preference for the other bold ones) in the wiki page and some other comments:

### tz or timezone
I am not sure, if this is a good name in general, as we can have time stamps given as UTC, really related to a time zone, local time (without DST), mean solar time, true solar time, ...?  This is not meant as a philosophical comment, but is an everyday problem from my experience, so we should be able to handle all of these formats (at least in the future, for now maybe it is ok to define, that all times should be given as UTC). 
Maybe `time_reference` (it should be decided by a native speaker ;-)? 

### dni_et
I like extra, should be used as index for all extraterrestrial irradiances. not sure if "direct" is a good name for extraterrestrial irradiance. However it is important to separate "normal", "horizontal" or "poa" extraterrestrial for some models (this was often a source of error for me, because I didn't cached which one was meant in the models).  

### global, direct and diffuse in general
Maybe it is better to have a more general rule, e.g to to use `g` (global), `d` (diffuse) and `b` (direct or beam) and than an index. Indices could be `normal`, `hor` (horizontal), `tilted` and `poa` (which is a special case, if it is not clear / not important if it is tilted or normal, e.g. for single axis tracking).
However, I am not sure on this rule, because the names would be different from the usual ones like `DNI` (would be `b_normal`) , `GHI` (would be `g_hor`), ... (but it would be more systematic).

There is an environment variable to override the per-user config file location: `$DDSCLIENT_CONF`.
The most straight forward way to share a config file between two users would be to set this environment variable to the same location(outside of the global config file).
If this environment variable is not set it falls back to `~/.ddsclient`.
If I check only in the fallback case I think users will be able to share service account config files.

__Summary__
```
If $DDSCLIENT_CONF is set
   do not check permissions
else
   check permissions on ~/.ddsclient
```
  
__Example Shared Service Config__
So in the case of HARDAC where all members of a lab might want to use the same service account.
They could do something like this:
```
export DDSCLIENT_CONF=/somelab/ddsclient.conf
```
If they always used this service account this they could just add it to their `.bash_profile`.

Any thoughts on fully committing to signed types for all buffer/array values? (itemsize, ndim, shape)

Having a mix of signed/unsigned can be accident-prone. E.g. if a user commits a cast while multiplying a negative stride with an unsigned `itemsize`, they're going to have a bad time. Having all signed values would also be nicely consistent, both internally and with Python/Numpy APIs.

(This may, however, raise additional backward compatibility issues.)

@lostintangent In general, our aim for table output was to be as automated/generated as possible. Currently, it picks the top-level attributes of objects (excluding things like ids as they're long and less useful in a table).
The thinking was that if users want specific columns/rows, a `--query` can be provided to get the exact table output you want.

Automated table output is preferred because in the future, the CLI will support multiple service API versions. These API versions could return different response objects. By making table output dynamic and automated, it would 'just work'.

There are commands where the default table format isn't useful so we've augmented these commands.
For example https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/master/src/command_modules/azure-cli-resource/azure/cli/command_modules/resource/commands.py#L36.

# Installation instruction
## Package azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices
You can install the package `azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices` of this PR using the following command:
	`pip install "git+https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python@restapi_auto_locationbasedservices/resource-manager#egg=azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices&subdirectory=azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices"`

You can build a wheel to distribute for test using the following command:
	`pip wheel --no-deps "git+https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python@restapi_auto_locationbasedservices/resource-manager#egg=azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices&subdirectory=azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices"`

If you have a local clone of this repository, you can also do:

- `git checkout restapi_auto_locationbasedservices/resource-manager`
- `pip install -e ./azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices`


Or build a wheel file to distribute for testing:

- `git checkout restapi_auto_locationbasedservices/resource-manager`
- `pip wheel --no-deps ./azure-mgmt-locationbasedservices`

I approve of renaming nurseries. When I first looked at Trio I had a little trouble understanding how nurseries worked, and I think that was mostly because of the name. The problem is not that the name is cute, it's that it doesn't have the right connotations. The name "nursery" suggests "a place where tasks/coroutines start out". Compare with memory management, we use the name "[nursery](http://www.memorymanagement.org/glossary/n.html#term-nursery-space)" to mean "a region of memory containing recently created objects".

But Trio tasks don't just *start* inside nurseries, they spend their *entire lifetime* inside the nursery. And the most important feature of nurseries is how they handle the *end* of the lifetime of their tasks: in particular, control doesn't exit the nursery's `with` statement until all the tasks created in that nursery have finished (either by all of them exiting normally, or by one of them raising an exception and the nursery cancelling the others). It's this property that allows us to reason about properties of code running in Trio, as described in Nathaniel's article "[Notes on structured concurrency](https://vorpus.org/blog/notes-on-structured-concurrency-or-go-statement-considered-harmful/)".

So whatever name is chosen, I think that it would be helpful to beginners if that name hinted at this lifetime (or synchronization/joining) property. Maybe the name **lifetime** would work.

The corresponding concept in normal (synchronous) structured programming is the "[block](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_(programming))" and this has a useful double meaning because a nursery's `with` statement [*blocks*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blocking_(computing)) (waits) until all its tasks have finished before exiting. So maybe a name like **lifetime block** or **parallel block** or **async block** would have the right connotations. ("Async block" is a near-rhyme with "[basic block](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basic_block)", which also has good connotations since it's the unit of code which compilers use to reason about the properties of code.)

I don't like the name "scope" because I associate that with controlling the binding of *names* (for example in "[lexical scope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scope_(computer_science)#Lexical_scoping)" and "[dynamic scope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scope_(computer_science)#Dynamic_scoping)") and it doesn't suggest anything about the lifetime of tasks to me.

I just poked a bit more at what would be involved in letting Trio's existing IO manager code "run under" a foreign event loop.

Apparently it's totally fine to call `epoll_ctl` from one thread while another thread is blocked in `epoll_wait`. From [a post on LKML](http://linux.derkeiler.com/Mailing-Lists/Kernel/2006-03/msg00084.html):

> > Suppose thread B calls epoll_wait and blocks before thread D calls
epoll_ctl. Is it safe to do so? Will thread B be notified for the
event submitted by thread D?

> Using the interfaces this way is pretty much their entire point. They'd be
almost useless if you couldn't use them in this way.

I know that the same is also true for IOCP, and I suspect (but have not verified) that it is true for kqueue.

Let's pretend for a moment that it's true for all of our primitives. (The Windows loop is actually much more complex right now, but as part of this exercise we're going to imagine we've switching Windows to using IOCP alone and gotten rid of `select`.)

Then we could split `handle_io` up, into two operations: one that just does the low-level blocking syscall, and returns an opaque object (e.g., the raw return value from the blocking syscall), and another that takes this opaque value and issues calls to `reschedule` etc. When running under a foreign event loop, we'd spawn a thread which does something like:

```python
while True:    
    opaque_object = handle_io_top_half(timeout=infinite)
    foreign_loop.call_soon_threadsafe(handle_io_bottom_half, opaque_object)
```

Simultaneously, we'd also run a scheduler loop, as a task under the foreign event loop:

```python
# Imagine this is unrolled into a callback-based state machine running under Qt or whatever
while True:
    await wait_runnable_tasks_queue_is_not_empty()
    batch = runnable_tasks_queue.popall()
    for task in batch:
        run task for one tick
```

This would require tweaking `reschedule` a bit so it can wake up the scheduler if necessary, but that's easy (at least in principle).

Now: if you call `reschedule` from inside the main thread, that should work fine. So operations like `queue.put_nowait` should work fine, either from Qt context or Trio context.

What if you do something that involves I/O, like `wait_readable`? Well, it mutates the IO manager's internal state, but that's all within the main thread, so it's OK. It also call `epoll_ctl` or similar to mutate the underlying OS's I/O watch state... but that's safe to do from any thread, according to our original assumption. And then when it reports back, that ends up calling `reschedule` in the main thread, which as noted should be fine.

Hmm... actually, as written above we have a small problem, because currently `handle_io_bottom_half` is responsible for resetting events that have happened (e.g. calling `epoll_ctl` to remove an fd from the watch set after the task that was waiting for it wakes up). So if we immediately call `epoll_wait` again, we'll report duplicate events. Two options: move the event resetting into `handle_io_top_half`, or else make sure that we don't schedule the next call to `handle_io_top_half` until the previous call to `handle_io_bottom_half` finishes. The latter is probably simpler all around.

So... that's a nice story. What are the gaps between this and reality?

* [ ] Need to check if kqueue is actually thread-safe in the necessary sense
* [ ] Need some serious work to figure out if/how can make the Windows IOManager play nicely with this (switch all the way to IOCP (see #52)? Some up with some wacky thread-safe epoll-like API that actually uses `select` underneath?)
* [ ] If we split the current `handle_io` into `handle_io_top_half` + `handle_io_bottom_half`, will that mess up performance in regular no-foreign-event-loop mode? (Maintaining two copies of the event loop logic is not very appealing.)
* [ ] Same question for making `reschedule` something that can hook into a foreign event loop to invoke the scheduler
* [ ] What would be required to unwind the core of `run` to work like this? How would *that* affect performance?
* [ ] What would the public API look like for this, for those implementing foreign event loop wrappers? For end users?

> But one concrete thing that I think is probably not the right tradeoff if we do decide to do this is using a dict for arguments. Particularly when you have typed arguments, requiring that they also be passed by name in all cases is overkill... so continuing to allow for use of positional arguments (ie, by making the list of types into `[('my_field': MyFieldType)])` or similar would be preferable.

I totally expected to have an extended discussion about the interface and/or impl before getting merged! I can see typed datatypes being very cool and I want to help ensure that code that lots of stuff depends on is still code that we should feel free to reach in and improve.

I hadn't remembered until you said this, but I have definitely seen the utility of not having field names, if each field is then required to have its own distinct type wrt the other fields of the `typed_datatype`. (This reminds me again of how someone mentioned `@rule`s could just be types in themselves in #5580.) This would encourage/require differentiating arguments not by name, but by type, which would then mean that usage context is canonicalized in the type itself, and not just in the name of the field with that type. This is a good thing, because then we can build up a hierarchy of types that add context to primitives like `PathGlobs` and `Snapshot`, which is good for the same reasons that types are good. People don't seem to like wrapper classes for some reason but I find it much easier to follow code with thin wrapper types that provide their own usage context compared to e.g. two different fields of the snapshot type, differentiated only by name. A quick example:
```python
class SomeTypeOfRequest(typed_datatype('SomeTypeOfRequest', {
  'binary_snapshot': Snapshot,
  'source_snapshot': Snapshot,
})):
  pass
```

What assumptions are we making about the two fields? How do we know the `'binary_snapshot'` contains a binary? If we changed the way we generate the value passed to `'binary_snapshot'`, and the path to the binary changed (for example), we would then have to find everywhere we interpret a snapshot as containing a binary, and modify those sites to manipulate the result correctly (basically: field names are more likely to change over time than type names). If instead, we did:
```python
class BinarySnapshot(typed_datatype('BinarySnapshot', {
  'snapshot': Snapshot,
  'binary_path_globs': PathGlobs,
}):

  def get_binaries_path_stats(self):
    # ...


class SourceSnapshot(typed_datatype('SourceSnapshot', {
  'snapshot': Snapshot,
}):
  # maybe we don't need to do anything here,
  # but it's clear what this is supposed to represent
  pass

class SomeTypeOfRequest(typed_datatype('SomeTypeOfRequest', {
  'binary_snapshot': BinarySnapshot,
  'source_snapshot': SourceSnapshot,
}):
  pass
```

We could even make the `get_binaries_path_stats()` into an `@rule` which could return another wrapper type. The reason I showed the above sample is that if we allow ourselves to be liberal with wrapper types, then we could remove the field names:
```python
class SomeTypeOfRequest(typed_datatype('SomeTypeOfRequest', (BinarySnapshot, SourceSnapshot)):
  pass

req = SomeTypeOfRequest(BinarySnapshot(...), SourceSnapshot(...))
# this would work as a result of some (probably simple) metaprogramming
# we could apply a similar technique as Task.stable_name() to generate the accessor name
# (that would then mean that all fields of some type would be accessed
# using the same field name, which is extremely interesting)
print(req.binary_snapshot)
```

But if we are allowing multiple fields of the same type, I wouldn't want to be able to invoke the constructor with any positional arguments whatsoever, that seems to me to defeat the purpose of type checking entirely -- I absolutely don't trust myself to always put positional arguments in the right order, and if multiple fields can have the same type we're back where we started (speaking personally). Thinking about it more, I find the idea of requiring distinct types *really* interesting, especially if we had magic property accessors generated from type names (totally not required -- but I really don't want to use `.0`, `.1`, etc like rust tuple structs, I find it absurdly opaque).
