No, that's exactly the problem. It's looking for `<buildroot>/src/go/foo/graph`, but that dir is actually at `<buildroot>/src/go/src/foo/graph`.

Your example repo might be too simple, I'll add a couple more imports to mirror mine and see if I can repro it. But from my debugging, it looks like at least part of the problem might be in the implementation of `GoLocalSource.local_import_path`. The Pants source root (aka target base) is the GOPATH, not GOPATH/src. E.g., if I apply this diff I get a lot further (but then choke on some 3rdparty issue):

```
--- a/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/targets/go_local_source.py
+++ b/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/targets/go_local_source.py
@@ -32,7 +32,9 @@ class GoLocalSource(GoTarget):
     :type: :class:`pants.build_graph.address.Address`
     :raises: `ValueError` if the address does not reside within the source root.
     """
-    return cls.package_path(source_root, address.spec_path)
+    # Note that the regular pants source root will be the GOPATH, and the root for the purpose
+    # of computing package paths is the `src` subdir of that source root.
+    return cls.package_path(os.path.join(source_root, 'src'), address.spec_path)
```

@mattharrison If I'm understanding correctly, your example code doesn't address this use case. This issue is about proxying _over an HTTPS proxy connection_. I.e. the proxy URL is itself an https - the connection between the client and the proxy is itself TLS/SSL-encrypted. Squid is an example of a proxy server [with this feature](http://wiki.squid-cache.org/Features/HTTPS#Encrypted_browser-Squid_connection). You code changes the proxy URL so it becomes proxy-over-http.

All that said, IMO, proxying over HTTPS is ill-advised in almost all cases, and actually decreases security. When people want to use it, it is almost always a result of misunderstanding (not being aware of how CONNECT works to maintain security when proxying https over http proxy). Unfortunately, misunderstanding or not, there are business environments where proxy-over-https is required by policy.

OK, I looked again, and AFAICT the answer is actually that they are different, it's just hard to tell by eyeballing :-). Part of what's confusing here is that patsy adjusts the splines to keep the nominal degrees of freedom, so if you say `df=6, include_intercept=False` then it creates a spline with 7 degrees of freedom and then discards one, while if you say `df=6, include_intercept=True` then it creates a spline with 6 degrees of freedom directly -- so either way, the design matrices end up with the same number of dimensions. (This is done partly because it's a reasonable interpretation, and partly because R does it this way and patsy's `bs()` is designed to be numerically compatible with R's `bs()` to facilitate transferring models.)

But at least when I do it, the matrices actually are different, and look like how I'd expect:

The `page_size` is only used internally when a `Result` or as in this case a `QueryResult` object is iterated over.  `page_size` is how this library provides you the ability to batch your iteration for large data sets so as not to blow out your memory.  Under the covers the `Result` iteration uses `skip` and `limit` to change your initial query to perform this batching.  In other words, if you are iterating over a `Result` you may be making multiple http requests to Cloudant to satisfy the iteration results.  That is why it is generally not a good idea to have `skip` and `limit` inside your query if you plan to iterate over the `Result`.  In fact you should get a raised exception.

`page_size` is meaningless when it comes to `Result` slicing or indexing as in `test[:]` or `test[1500]`.  `Result` slicing also uses `skip` and `limit` but any `Result` slice or `Result` index is only going to make a single http request to Cloudant to get your data.

The difference between using `Query` and the database convenience method `get_query_result` is that `Query` is callable and will return the actual JSON response from your query whereas `get_query_result` by default will return a `Result` object which is a wrapper that tacks on the indexing, slicing, and iteration niceties mentioned earlier.  That `Result` object does not contain the query result initially, all it does is allows you to programmatically manipulate your query so that you can use it Pythonically and then in turn calls the `Query` callable.  You can set `raw_result=True` for `get_query_result` and then it will behave the same as if you called the `Query` callable directly with your query.

If you are looking to use a `bookmark` then you are looking to make multiple separate queries, each as a single request to Cloudant and you are looking to manage the resulting sets of data yourself.  The `bookmark` query parameter is how Cloudant (not this library) provides you the ability to batch your text search results into manageable chunks.  So, in this case using a `Result` object is probably not what you want to be doing.  Instead you should follow the Cloudant documentation on [queries](https://docs.cloudant.com/search.html#queries), use the `raw_result=True` option on `get_query_result`, call it as often as you need to using a different `bookmark` each time and then manage the result sets as necessary.

Prior art: other python implementations of RGB->LCH conversion. 

### skimage (the benchmark)
We already use the **[skimage.color](https://github.com/mapbox/rio-color/blob/master/rio_color/operations.py#L60-L77)** which works on arrays of shape `(..., ..., 3)` with values scaled 0..1. Doing the conversion requires going through the LAB color space.  So far this is the benchmark.

### colormath 👎 
The **[colormath](http://python-colormath.readthedocs.org)** module is excellent for exploring and validating color conversions on single values. Not so great at optimizing the calculation on large arrays since it uses python objects. It's really designed for usage like:

```python
from colormath.color_objects import LCHabColor
from colormath.color_objects import sRGBColor
rgb = sRGBColor(0.5, 0.8, 0.1)
convert_color(rgb, LCHabColor)
# LCHabColor(lch_l=74.70319882217456,lch_c=85.60523822265391,lch_h=124.3276180394683)
```
Converting that single value takes about 59.2 µs. For the largish raster above (8192 x 8192) that would take an hour assuming we could vectorize it effectively. So performance is just not even in the right ballpark. Still a useful library for understanding colorspaces though!

### Other options

* Dig into the skimage implementation and try to optimize
* Implement our own in cython or vectorized numpy ops. Definitely possible but would take some time.
* Find a good RGB<->LAB conversion and take it to/from LAB->LCH-> adjust saturation->LAB ourselves
* opencv
* PIL: http://pillow.readthedocs.org/en/3.1.x/reference/ImageCms.html
* http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7530627/hcl-color-to-rgb-and-backward
* http://stackoverflow.com/a/16020102/519385

I'll update this ticket as we explore other avenues...

<hr>

Overall, I'm surprised at the lack of performance-oriented, array-based colorspace libraries in the Python world.

As per discussion w/ @perrygeo and @batpad , I would like to take work on sdk-py for Analytics API ahead.  Based on reading through existing sdk-py, found the workflow as following:
 - A session is created initially using the Mapbox access token. The existing session is used if there is cache present.
 -  The next action is to validate all the input parameters.
 -  Once these are validated, the request is made to the host `api.mapbox.com` passing the input parameters. And the response is sent back to the client.

[Analytics API](https://www.mapbox.com/api-documentation/?language=cURL#analytics) provides usage statistics for different services provided by Mapbox. It receives parameters as following: 
 - **resourceType** : The resource being requested. This can be validated against a list of valid resource types : `accounts , tokens , tilesets or styles`
 - **username** : The user to whom resource belongs.
 - **id** (optional) : The id for the resource(token id, mapId, styleId respectively). In the case of accounts this isn't required. This will be in form of a string. To validate this parameter, should it checked for a string?
 - **period** (optional) : 2 comma separated dates as [ISO formatted strings](https://www.mapbox.com/api-documentation/?language=cURL#dates) . This should be validated for ISO formatted strings, first date earlier than second date, difference between two dates should be less than a year.

**Note** :  A secret access token is required which has a scope of `analytics::read` to create the session.

Response: This returns `timestamps, period and different services` in form of JSON. Services and usage report depend on the resource type one request for. 
 - **Account** : It returns the usage report of different services accessed across all the tokens belonging to user.
 - **tokens** : It returns the usage report of different services accessed using the token id provided.
 - **tilesets** : It returns the usage report of a Map with given MapId in different services
 - **styleId**: It returns the usage report of style with a given StyleId in different services.

I have a general query that if the input parameters need to be validated at SDK end as well, in case when they are getting validated at API level? For example, As I mentioned `Period` validation above, if we give wrong parameter to API, the response will contain error which we can directly pass to the client back. Or what are the right practices?

@perrygeo @batpad Let me know if I missed out on something or understood something incorrectly. Would be glad to hear your thoughts on above.
Thank you!

@dset0x The broker only ensures that no messages are lost, that the load is balanced and that some messages can be delayed/scheduled. it does not know about the format+content of the messages. This is handled by celery (celery is NOT the broker btw) and if we just set `msgpack` as a message format and wildly modify some slots, than even celery is not aware of our problem.

But (here comes the good part): We could introduce a protocol version (which includes setting the message format to something like `invenio-msgpack-1.0`) and kinda solve the problem IF celery does the following: "do not touch messages with unkown protocols and leave them in the queue". We have to check if this is satisfied.

"supporting and testing" -- I was indeed looking at removing 3.3 and 3.4 also from Trove classifiers.  If we claim to support some Python version, then we'd better also be testing it.

... but its default Python version is 2.7.  Hence I thought that my proposal of supporting 2.7 and 3.5 may be enough.

- E.g. Ubuntu 14.04 LTS is Python 2.7 and 3.4. (Supported until mid 2019.)

- E.g. latest Ubuntu 16.04 LTS is Python 2.7 and 3.5.  

Covering 2.7 and 3.5 would seem the safest, provided that e.g. services on Ubuntu14 or CentOS7 stick to their Python 2 defaults.  If they would like to move to Python 3, then it would be nice to have Python 3.4 supported for some years to come indeed...but a reply "please stick to default 2.7 or else use Docker" would seem acceptable to me from the Invenio project point of view.   

> dropping Python 2.7 support [...] Right now I think it's primarily xrootd python bindings which are blocking us.

For this, there are two options.

The quickest option is to do something like what the SRL model does to incorporate the verb indicator.  The `DatasetReader` adds a `SequenceLabelField` with binary values indicating whether each token is the verb to find arguments for: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/ed37843d528b0902d73e4d5dde931812fd65835d/allennlp/data/dataset_readers/semantic_role_labeling.py#L277

The model then takes that field, embeds it, and concatenates it manually to the word embedding:
https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/ed37843d528b0902d73e4d5dde931812fd65835d/allennlp/models/semantic_role_labeler.py#L119-L122

You could do this for each binary feature you want, but just skip the feature embedding.

If you have a lot of binary features you want to add, though, this could get annoying.  So the second option is to write your own `TokenIndexer` that converts word tokens into feature vectors using whatever feature extractors you want to use, and a `TokenEmbedder` that just passes through the given feature vector.  If you want to go this route, I'm happy to look at a work-in-progress PR to let you know if you're doing it the right way.

I think of the ambiguously placed gap as an independent variant (insGGA), which in HGVS would use the 3'-shifted location: g.73613070_ 73613071insGGA.  So in my opinion, the rightmost placement of the alignment gap is preferable, and NM_015120.4n.150G>C  --> g.73613035.  The transcript has ...TGGAG > TGGAC, and the genome can too.  Otherwise the genome changing at 73613032 assumes that insGGA has already been applied in its leftmost position.  I see your point that either is mathematically valid, and using the same alignment, the transform would be reversible -- but at least for the sake of consistency, I like using the rightmost interpretation of the alignment gap.  (Should we take it up with HGVS?)

Substitutions that may or may not overlap ambiguously placed alignment gaps are about the trickiest case I've encountered.  I have a trick for making sure that they are detected -- going from genome to transcript, but it might carry over in a straightforward way to transcript to genome.  YMMV.  

Before translating coordinates, I look for ambiguously placed gaps in the transcript alignment -- and I modify the alignment to have a double-sided gap over the entire ambiguous region.  This is an easy modification in UCSC's internal representation of alignments as a series of alignment block coordinates on target and query.  In this case, instead of skipping 3 transcript bases and 0 genome bases, the coordinates skip over 42 transcript bases and 39 genome bases.  

Then it's trivial to detect whether a variant's projection is affected by an alignment gap -- no need to go searching the neighborhood, or taking chances with detecting the interaction or not.  

Having determined that the variant is somehow affected by a possibly ambiguously placed alignment gap,  I apply the variant change to the ambiguous region sequence, which has the same effect as using the rightmost placement (my preference) of the alignment gap.  In case the variant is an insertion or deletion that will need right-shifting itself, I trim matching bases from the modified ambiguous regions of genome and transcript, first from the left to get the rightmost position, then from the right, and that yields the right-shifted position and minimal base change.

Complicated, maybe -- but consistent in the face of differing alignment gap placements.  Written in a bit of a hurry, so if I have stated something unclearly, let me know and I'll try to make it more coherent. :)

Actually the logic is : the key of the `WeakKeyDictionary` is an instance of the `regions.Region` class and it's value is the value of the attribute . We use weak dictionary so that the key value pair exists until the instance is alive, hence prevents memory leakage.
We store it in this way because the descriptor classes are static to it's owner class. 
The problem with this arises only when the instances are unhashable, which is not the case for `regions.Region` (according to the link, you provided).

@greysteil `Mapping` is an [abstract base class](https://docs.python.org/3/library/abc.html), or ABC. It does not need to be inherited directly; Python does some metaclass magic so an `isinstance` check against them only checks for the *interface*, not the inheritance chain. By checking against `Mapping` we ensure `val` has a dict-like behaviour for reading, which prettytoml table classes have. It should cover the `AbstractTable` case (although there’s no harm checking for it).

@JosefAssad thanks for the clear response -- the docker use case is a bit different I think, just because docker containers are usually atomic and many users are installing with `--system` inside a container. The use case of `pew` invocation is an interesting point I've been thinking about since you mentioned it -- we currently have some logic to find executables, but I don't believe we are applying this to our calls to pew presently, which is one of the reasons it won't work just by changing to the project directory.

For context, `pipenv run` currently works with the following code:

```python
def inline_activate_virtualenv():
    try:
        activate_this = which('activate_this.py')
        with open(activate_this) as f:
            code = compile(f.read(), activate_this, 'exec')
            exec(code, dict(__file__=activate_this))
    # Catch all errors, just in case.
    except Exception:
        click.echo(
            u'{0}: There was an unexpected error while activating your virtualenv. Continuing anyway…'
            ''.format(crayons.red('Warning', bold=True)),
            err=True
        )
```

Note that `which()` uses `project.virtualenv_location` which is the same as `pipenv --venv` which is also dependent on a call to `pew`.

Haha the multi-build stuff has been around for about [slightly more than a year][msb]. I'm far from an expert myself, but my take on it depends on what's the goal with having the Dockerfile here:

If it's about being more generally useful, this can go big very quickly (think [docker-library/python][p]), but that's a lot of work (and I'm not sure we would be able to go very far on usefulness due to system dependencies). 

And if it's about a quick "getting started" kind of thing: keep the onbuild rules, but be more explicit about it and its gotchas. That to me means appending `-onbuild` to the image name on dockerhub and having targeted documentation giving usage directions and how to evolve from it when the time comes.

Were it up to me I'd go for the latter as it seems to strike a better balance between effort and usefulness.

[p]: https://github.com/docker-library/python
[msb]: https://docs.docker.com/release-notes/docker-ce/#17050-ce-2017-05-04

Ok. I am sorry to hear that. But I think there have been multiple instances where you raised issues, and these got addressed in the way you proposed! And I highly appreciate your input and the discussions.

And then there are instances where I have a strong opinion, like with this issue. Let me try to expand why. I think one of the core value propositions of WAMP is decoupling and abstraction. If we compromise on that, we might loose the whole point of WAMP. That is my general concern.

Let's take your proposal above: it _seems_ simple to shortcut calls for endpoints which reside in the same client first. But e.g. what about pattern-based registrations, shared registrations, call cancelling, and other advanced features? You would end up with a router embedded in the client ultimately. And implement that functionality in each and every Autobahn client library. Because next would be adding client-internal broker functionality ("Why can't I have client-interal PubSub brokering?"). And then Python isn't enough, but people would ask to replicate this in AutobahnJS as well.

 > to fulfill some abstract paradigmas, philosphies or moral views, fine

What moral views? "some abstract paradigms": yes, decoupling, language and deployment independence. If you disagree with these, then WAMP isn't for you.

Maybe it's a question of granularity: making each and every tiny little function in an application into a registered WAMP procedure is probably going too far. WAMP tries to adress the "microservice granularity", not language-specific, function level.

> But my impression so far has been, unless YOU see and encounter a problem, there is no problem.

I think that's unfair, and not supported by looking at the history of changes to WAMP etc that directly resulted from community feedback and proposals.

> Maybe in a year you will realize that 80% of the traffic hopping over your router could be completely ommited and this will eventually get implemented.

Then throw away all of WAMP and implement a monolithic Python application. That will have the least overhead!

> At this point iI'm just tired of investing hours and hours trying to propose meaningful improvements just to get them rejected in a dictator-like fashion for "reasons". 

Feel free to use something else that better fits your requirements.

I think "start" is gone from recent incarnations? I think it was just fired when you "started running your Component" .. but yes is should probably have a matchin "done" or "stopped" or similar if we want it?

I think "join" / "leave" and "connect"  / "disconnect" are the right pairs of words? (or it would want to be "joined" and "left").

IIRC we had semantics that the future returned from `Component.start()` would fire when the thing was "done" (which is after all on-leave listeners are completed) so that points to having a "done" event mirroring "ready" (i.e. "ready" fires after join + all listeners, "done" fires after "leave" and all listeners)...but looking at it now maybe that would be more clear if the events were "join", "joined" and "leave", "left"? And then keep "connect" / "disconnect".

Looking in the code, "start" currently fires as soon as something calls `.start` on a Component .. so I guess a matching "end" event would probably make sense too (which fires at the same time as the future returned from `Component.start`).

Then the overall picture would be something like: start -> connected -> join -> joined/ready -> leave -> left -> disconnected -> end. The "connected -> ... -> disconnected" part would be a loop while doing retrys or re-connects and so "end" would only fire after we'd given up on all transports and retry/reconnect attempts (or were told stop).

Was there a particular reason you chose to use `lambda A, x: np.dot(np.linalg.pinv(A), x)` over `np.linalg.lstsq`? I think `lstsq` may have better broadcasting properties, and it also may be faster (fewer temporaries allocated, fewer ops) and more numerically stable.

As for (2) (which I actually only just read the code for), reverse-mode autodiff generally requires keeping the forward values around in memory, so the memory usage should scale as \Theta(k) there. If you can rewrite code to generate fewer temporaries on the forward pass then of course memory usage would be reduced, but those temporaries can't be garbage collected until the backward pass is done. An optimizing compiler could in principle rewrite that code for you, but autograd doesn't do any rewriting; it just follows the forward pass (keeping references to any temporaries generated so they can't be garbage collected).

Hi @emmett9001, I was assuming a code like this:

```python
for x in consumer:
    process(x)
```

I wonder if during the rebalance this code just goes on. The rebalance lock seems to be used only by `_rebalance` itself and the `close` method. Let's assume this run (offsets are represented by the pair <partition id, offset>, we start with a consumer group with one node and we add a second one later):

```
node1: offset <1,1> consumed
node1: offset <2,1> consumed
node1: offset <1,2> consumed || rebalance (a new consumer joins the group), offsets are committed now
node1: offset <2,2> consumed || rebalance in progress
node1: offset <1,3> consumed || rebalance finished: part1 belongs to node1, part2 to node2
node1: offset <1,3> consumed <-- duplicate processing for partition 1
node2: offset <2,2> consumed <-- duplicate processing also for partition 2
```

As far as I've understood this could actually happen because the rebalance is done in a parallel thread and there is no lock for the `consume` method.

About the issue #167 I still feel like that's the correct behavior when `auto_commit_enable` is `True` but I would rather give the caller the responsibility to commit the offsets when it is `False`. I wonder if this would be possible by just adding a pre-rebalance callback.

Hey - to answer the title question directly, you can use [the get_requests_count](http://cassiopeia.readthedocs.org/en/latest/cassiopeia/riotapi.html#cassiopeia.riotapi.get_requests_count) method to keep track of how many API calls have been used in your cass code.

> My question here is - how many "api requests" have I just made?
It's hard to tell exactly, because Cass is always doing its level best to ensure that as few calls as possible are being used in the long run. In this particular case (only considering the lines of code in your post), you can't have used more than 2 API calls (plus a static-data call, but they don't count towards your rate limit).

If you're using the Lazy load policy, Cass will use an API call to retrieve the Summoner object for Dyrus when you first call `dyrus.summoner` (but will cache it and won't do it again next time). In some cases, it won't make any calls at all here.

Cass will also use an API call for the `ranked_stats(season=Season.season_5)` call to get those stats. This one **will not** be cached by Cass automatically, so be careful not to call this method in a tight loop.

None of the code in your second block will make any API calls, as you've already loaded all that data through the calls above.

> since everything really came from the summoner object, "dyrus"?
The Cass object hierarchy does not mirror the Riot API's exactly. Some of the methods you can call on a Summoner, Match, or similar object are convenience methods that will make another API call to get information that normally isn't contained in that object. An example of this is `summoner.ranked_stats()`. In the Riot API, ranked stats aren't contained in the Summoner object. Cassiopeia provides this as a shortcut to calling `riotapi.get_ranked_stats(summoner)`. In general, though not always, methods of Cass objects will make additional API calls, whereas attributes usually won't (unless that data will be cached after the first call).

> One idea was while keeping everything the same, I use "to_json" attribute for champstat.
This might be a useful exercise to help understand the differences between what is available with a Cass object and what is directly served by the Riot API, but this will **not** have an effect on the number of calls you'll have to make.

The entire point of Cass is to make dealing with the JSON that the Riot API sends easy and efficient. By outputting the JSON and getting data from that afterwards, you're defeating the purpose of using Cass in the first place.

A Cass object will _never_ make another API call to get you data that is already contained within that object's JSON. It only ever makes additional calls to get information that it doesn't already have.

> btw, please let me know if this kind of question needs to be asked somewhere else. I'm not even sure if this is a question specific to Cassiopeia or more of a broader python/api question ...

No worries, this is the correct place to ask. What you're asking is definitely within the scope of Cass, rather than Python/API. We also have a Slack group for people who are interested in a quicker feedback loop for Cass and other Meraki Analytics tools. Let me know if you'd like to be added to it.

To sum this all up: A lot of thought went into trying to ensure that Cass is always using as few API calls as possible. It may be valuable to read about the [load policies](http://cassiopeia.readthedocs.org/en/latest/loadpolicy.html#load-policies) in Cass if you're concerned about optimizing call usage. Each policy makes different assumptions about how you're using the data, but we're reasonably certain that each policy makes the best possible use of your rate limit given those assumptions.

Following the comment above, my idea is that `ParameterHandler.from_schema` should always expect the full schema, like:

```python
class RequestSchema(colander.MappingSchema):
    body = BodySchema()
    querystring = QuerySchema()
    header = HeaderSchema()
```

Doing so, it doesn't need to know about validators. But this approach needs an intermediate step aware of validators between `CorniceSwagger._extract_operation_from_view` and `ParameterHandler.from_schema` that transforms schemas in the case of `colander_body_validator`.
The transform step enables also some neat features, like adding application wide extra schema nodes (for example headers handled by the authentication layer). What do you think?

Hmm, seems like the output of the conv_transpose operation might not be the correct shape. If I understand correctly you are trying to go from [batch, 7, 7, 16] up to [batch, 16, 16, 1] using a filter size of (16, 16), SAME padding, and stride of 1. If I understand conv_transpose correctly the actual output shape of that conv_transpose operation will be (batch, 7, 7, 1) and not (batch, 16, 16, 1).

The way I've thought about conv_transpose is I think about what output shape I want. Then I think about what parameters to a normal conv layer would turn that output into the input (so how could we go from [batch, 16, 16, 1]  to [batch, 7, 7, 16]). Then I use those parameters in a conv_transpose to go from the input to output. So in this situation using a stride of 2, pad=VALID, and a filter size of (3,3) for your conv_transpose might do the trick.

Including an `encoding` field should be fine; I can support that change. But there are two significant problems with including the proposed `type` field:
 1. Requiring that all images for a given tag be of the same size and/or that each tag have a constant number of images across steps is a significant restriction of the API that we do not want to introduce. See comments on Google-internal change 
 161843301.
 2. Even if we accept that restriction—which I recommend that we not do—encoding the shape into the summary metadata would require that the shape be known _statically_, because the metadata is provided with the call to `tf.summary.tensor_summary`. This is definitely not acceptable: it means that `image.summary.op(tf.decode_png(input_data))` is illegal, it means that if you want to change some hyperparameters you need to recompute your graph, etc.

This sounds like an interesting problem, but I'm not sure its one nteract can solve. Nteract and all the kernels for nteract rely on the Jupyter messaging specification. My (rather limited) understanding is that the way Python handles package management is different than javascript, julia, and other languages for which there is a messaging-spec adhering kernel. the output order is based on message order, which corresponds (in IPython) to the order of `flush` calls. If there's pending output still waiting when a cell finishes, IPython calls: ```python sys.stdout.flush() sys.stderr.flush() ``` If you call flush yourself, you can influence the order: ipykernel 4.3 flushes asynchronously, so you will get some time-sensitive ordering if there's enough time between writes: Which also demonstrates that we don't merge the streams, so there's a newline on every switch because we put each stdout/stderr switch in divs. This is not an accurate representation of stream output in a terminal, so if you wanted to do a better job, you could do something with spans for the switch instead of divs. Alternately, you could completely ignore the difference between stdout and stderr, which is what a terminal does.

> Am I correct in thinking that nteract doesn't and won't support the double slash LaTeX delimiters (`\\(…\\)` and `\\[…\\]`; n.b.: the dollar signs are technically TeX delimiters and so are dispreferred by some)?

Dollar signs are the simplest to support, in terms of not conflicting the with markdown grammar whereas `\\[` is completely plausible in markdown and should be able to be written without being treated as `LaTeX`. That being said, the mathjax config we have for mathjax-electron does allow for `\\[` -- https://github.com/nteract/mathjax-electron/blob/master/resources/MathJax/config/electron.js

> How does this handle multiline LaTeX blocks defined with \begin{.*}…\end{.*}?

I don't think this does. This swaps out the markdown parser and renderer to not be commonmark anymore so that the entities we want, particularly `$$`. Everything else is up to the mathjax config from [mathjax-electron](https://github.com/nteract/mathjax-electron/blob/master/resources/MathJax/config/electron.js)

It appears that you don't have multiple App Servers, just 'SASApp', so it doesn't appear you're connecting to a different one with saspy then with EG, so you don'e need to specify the appserver key in your configuration definition.
But, you aren't able to assign the library that you can in EG.
So, I'm wondering if the difference could be that in EG you are connecting to a Pooled workspace server, not the regular workspace server? There can be different configurations for those, so maybe those libraries are available in the Pooled and not in the non-pooled ones. The other possibility with that is they are available to both, but it's a permission issue. I believe the pooled workspace servers are launched with specific credentials, whereas reguler workspace servers are launched with the individual users (yours) credentials. So, it could also be that those libraries are there but you're userid (on the workspace server) can't access them, while when connected to a pooled WS, the credentials it's running under does have permission to assign those libraries.

Can you check to see if that is the issue? I don't see any other reason for this from what I'm seeing.

Thanks,
Tom

Hi @jpapon 
The main problem we are trying to solve is RGB streaming in Full-HD. Our current SSE-optimized YUY to RGB conversion is still too taxing to keep 60 (or even 30) FPS and we want to avoid frame-drops. 
We checked jumping to AVX, but not everyone has AVX support at this point, so it would require dynamic CPUID identification and it is hard to do cross-platform. Obviously, on ARM the situation is even more dire (we are looking into potential NEON optimization). That's why OpenMP was chosen as a robust, cross-platform alternative. 
In addition, we know that both the colorizer and the projection code needs to be better optimized.

This is partly done by #302 and #337. You can now call handler functions with request objects directly:

    response = my_registered_tchannel_endpoint(Request("foo"))

But "foo" needs to the struct representing the arguments of the method. So, if you had,

    void putValue(1: string key, 2: string value)

You still need the `putValue_args(key, value)` struct passed in as the `Request.body` when calling the function directly. We currently don't expose a way to do that. I was considering something like the following for that:

    @tchannel.thrift.register(KeyValue)
    def putValue(request):
        # ...

    putValue.args("key", "value")   # builds a putValue_args object.

    # This will allow you to do:
    putValue(Request(body=putValue.args("key", "value"))

I'm open to other suggestions.

Adding more metadata is very simple. All it takes is adding (offset, dtype, count) tuples to the Spec.metadata dict. The reason why I did not do that (yet) is testing: Our software only writes a subset of the metadata, and I only implemented those (and the SPE specs are not very helpful in figuring stuff out unless one can try oneself). I guess, adding the additional entries from the filestore reader would take about ten minutes.

However, there is the question of naming: I stuck with the names from the specs. The filestore version uses more descriptive names.

Last but not least: The XML metadata in the SPE 3.0 spec describes the layout of binary data in the file, and not only some additional information. This would require a completely new reader class to implement.

There are two issues here, even though I think only one could be approached. Firstly, the git command could not be spawned as the system appears be out of resources. As GitPython spawns a process, it should have it's own limit of open files, so it appears to be the OS being unable to open any more files for the current user. This is something entirely out of GitPython's control.

However, GitPython just assumes that probably the git command was not found, and throws a `GitCommandNotFound` exception, which is wrong in this case. I believe this was implemented assuming it would be correct most of the time. Changing the name of said exception would be breaking, so I would rather not want to do it.

What would you want to see happen here ?

I would be cautious with piping 2D DICOM slices through standard DICOM to NIfTI conversion. Beyond the slice thickness problems you previously mentioned, the depth is usually computed as the cross-product of the row and column vectors, so the polarity is ambiguous. While viewing a single slice makes sense, one does not know the depth direction. In other words, are you viewing a slice from radiological (foot/anterior) or neurological (head/posterior) direction. I think this figure shows the issue
 https://www.nitrc.org/plugins/mwiki/index.php/mricrogl:MainPage#Neurological_versus_radiological_convention

It seems like there should be simple solution, but as I recall the way different vendors have implemented this makes it tricky.

Well, there are actually two issues here. 

One is that _we shouldn't be taking the package name from the file name_. According to the documentation, the filename isn't supposed to matter. It's just supposed to be a key into a dictionary containing the metadata, which includes the true package name. This is going to be important moving ahead when we handle channel collisions and namespaces properly. Still, until the transition is complete, we need the `rsplit` approach as a fallback.

Then there's the question of where the function should go. It seems to me `conda.install` is higher-level than `conda.resolve`, so it should probably go in the latter.

Both of those reasons point to this being a _method_ of the `Resolve` class:
```
def package_name(self, fn):
    rec = self.index.get(fn, None)
    return rec['name'] if rec else fn.rsplit('-',2)[0]
```

That's a good point.

Ultimately, we need to fail with an appropriate error message if a user inputs an invalid channel.  Maybe they fat-fingered something.  Maybe they pasted an extra character.  If they have other channels and fall back to other packages, they could go forward with operations "silently" if we allowed for 404s to pass generally.  So bottom line we need *some way* to verify that a channel is "somewhat" valid.

I'm open to the "has either" suggestion.  I can see two drawbacks to it right now though.

1) An identical url could be a valid channel on linux-64 but an invalid channel on linux-32.  This was actually the behavior I was hoping to avoid.
2) The logic for downloading repodata becomes much more complicated.  It's something that can't be simply parallelized anymore.  We need an extra processing step for 403/404s, to make sure we get a response from one url or the other.  I'd honestly rather just keep things as is than do that.

I had the same problem after updating conda. My .condarc file is:
```
channels:
- defaults
- https://conda.anaconda.org/r
- https://conda.anaconda.org/bioconda
```
As suggested by @mcg1969 , updating `get_rc_urls` as:
```
def get_rc_urls():
    if rc is None:
        return []
    if rc.get('channels') is None:
        return []
    if 'system' in rc['channels']:
        raise RuntimeError("system cannot be used in .condarc")
    return rc['channels']
```

works for me.

sudo bash Anaconda3-5.0.1-Linux-x86_64.sh     # with all defaults...

Then

    ls -ld anaconda3   
    # returns:      drwxr-x--- 22 root root 4096 Jan  2 12:57 anaconda3

This is what `mulitchannels` are for:
```bash
$ conda config --describe custom_multichannels
# custom_multichannels (map: list)
#   A multichannel is a metachannel composed of multiple channels. The two
#   reserved multichannels are 'defaults' and 'local'. The 'defaults'
#   multichannel is customized using the 'default_channels' parameter. The
#   'local' multichannel is a list of file:// channel locations where
#   conda-build stashes successfully-built packages.  Other multichannels
#   can be defined with custom_multichannels, where the key is the
#   multichannel name and the value is a list of channel names and/or
#   channel urls.
# 
# custom_multichannels: {}
```
As and example (apart from the default ones in the help text above), you can add
```yaml
custom_multichannels:
  bio-forge:
    - bioconda
    - conda-forge
```
to your `.condarc` which you can then use with
```bash
$ conda create -nsome-env -c bio-forge bwa
```

> So I'd suggest to have a look at the end-user API first: Does it cover all the use cases, in readable end-user code?

I think the best way to test this is to write some examples, e.g. an attention network, text prediction ala Karpathy, etc.

> Keeping some underlying parameters fixed can easily be solved by making them constants instead of shared variables.

But then how would you change their values during training? I might not want to perform gradient updates on a given variable, but that doesn't mean I don't want to change it at all :)

> The only use case that wouldn't be covered would be setting W to some expression that involves two shared variables, both of which should be trained, but only one of which should be regularized. I think this is exotic enough to not bother with it for now, following our design goal Pragmatism. People can always subclass a layer to accomplish more complex parameterization.

Agreed, I could live with that.

> I'd strongly vote for having get_all_params() to return the 'real' parameter variables by default, because that way most existing code would continue to work without changes. 

I think we need to reflect on nomenclature here. If we are going to refer to both real and virtual parameters as 'params', we are going to hopelessly confuse people. I agree that letting `get_all_params()` return the variables is the easiest and most straightforward thing to do, but I can't get behind a situation where `Layer.params` potentially contains expressions, and `get_all_params()` only returns shared variables. Referring to both of these as 'params' is a bad idea. Maybe we should call the latter 'variables' or something? That's not really satisfactory either I guess. 'real params' and 'virtual params' is too cumbersome, but this terminology will do nicely for this discussion at least.

Also let's keep in mind that we want to shield any users who do not wish to use this feature (i.e. the majority of them, most likely) from ever having to deal with any of this. From what's discussed so far I think this would be the case, but let's just keep it in mind nevertheless.

> I disagree. Layers give tags to 'virtual' parameters, and those should just apply to all 'real' variables contained within. Everything else is unnecessarily complex for now. (And as I said, there's always T.constant if it's just about trainability.)

By building a new Python interpreter with `PYMALLOC_DEBUG` set, I was able to see that the `name` string does become a dangling pointer after the `Py_DECREF`. In this build, the memory of deleted objects is specifically scribbled over at delete time:

```C
  if (PyUnicode_Check(oname)) {
    tmp=PyUnicode_AsASCIIString(oname);
    if (tmp==NULL) return NULL;
    name=PyString_AS_STRING(tmp);
	printf("\n$$$ Got unicode; name %s\n", name);
    Py_DECREF(tmp);
	printf("\n$$$ DECREF uni ; name %s\n", name);
  }
```

```python
>>> import Acquisition._Acquisition
[134190 refs]
>>> class X(object): pass
...
[134213 refs]
>>> x = X()
[134216 refs]
>>> w = Acquisition.ImplicitAcquisitionWrapper(x, None)
[134219 refs]
>>> Acquisition.aq_get(w, u'foo')

$$$ Got unicode; name foo

$$$ DECREF uni ; name ��������������������
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: foo
[134248 refs]
```

So it seems like basically luck is what's making the unicode case work now. I can't prove it's the also the cause of the segfault because I haven't trapped it under a debugger and it only happens pretty rarely in production---but we do run with `from __future__ import unicode_literals` so most calls to `getattr` would be passing unicode strings.

I'll work on a PR to fix this. Unfortunately, I'm not sure how to test for it; any suggestions would be welcome. Alas, it's going to be a bit bigger of a change than just copying the `name` string, because of needing to free it again, and there are so many `return` points in that function. It looks to me like a wrapper function will be the way to go, but I'm open to any other ideas.

It could be at either the construction of the `AttributeAnnotations` object or it could be deferred until first used, a-la `@Lazy` or the like.

But I think that even in applications that use multiple annotation keys, that's usually spread out over the code base, and it's unlikely that the same `AttributeAnnotations` object is reused. It's more often that each use site does something like `IAnnotations(object)['mykey']`...which is exactly the pattern that the `factory()` function encourages, so even for applications that use the *same* key in different places the underlying annotation adapter tends to get recreated. So it's hard to see how this can be a win without caching the `partial` somewhere external to the `AttributeAnnotations` object.

I think the conversation here has strayed a bit from the question at hand.  The question is what to do about the `treering_center` keyword when `transpose=True`.

I'm inclined to keep the behavior as is, since I think it matches the doc string, and I think the doc string is sensible.  I should add a bit of text to the doc string pointing out that the treering centers are in the pixel coordinates of the given image, even if `transpose=True`.  I.e. they are not also transposed.  Just the B/F effect.

Then it's the responsibility of the user to give the tree ring center in the coordinates of the image they actually give to Silicon, which seems reasonable.

Furthermore, ImSim will likely be the only user of this feature for the foreseeable future.  For now, the tree ring centers are random anyway, so the transpose is irrelevant.  And even if they ever become relevant (e.g. to match real lab-measured locations), they are implicitly in CCS coordinates in ImSim, which means that the current behavior would be the desired one anyway.

If you always rely on the requirements.txt file with constrained version then you never updates your dependencies unless your setup pyup or a process to upgrade your dependencies.

Previously we assumed that upgrading a dependency was a wish-able thing that we wanted to do as soon as possible. If it breaks we will detect it at last before doing the release because we run our release pull-request on the CI while tagging the list of dependencies.

It is fine to use pyup even if having pull-request every few hour is a bit annoying.

In anycase you should never use setup.py to constraint dependencies for a library because you can endup in a project where a lib relies on a version and another lib on another version. (It happens a lot especially requests for instance)

To prevent breaking developers environments we took some precautions:

- We have cronjob that run the CI on master periodically to tell us if a dependency failed.
- We have ci on each pull-request so each time we merge we know that the dependency set is right,
- If it break for someone contributing, they can file an issue and we handle it.
- Each release comes with a `requirements.txt` file that can be use to constraint pip dependencies versions and make sure to have reproducible installation (that we use with kinto-dist when releasing)

In Kinto we now have pyup so the `requirements.txt` file is always kept up-to-date.

I know people that use it to install the dependencies and in that case they use the last validated version.

Some comments: you're focusing on what kinto does for the users at first, stating what they want, but this might or might not be the case, depdending who your users are.

I proposed a few changes (in the pad and here):

```rst

We believe data belongs to the users, and not to the application authors. When writing an application, data should be made available from any device, connected or offline, and everything should be in sync.

Rather than spending a non-trivial amount of time and expertise on implementing all that (and then maintaining it !), you could use Kinto, that does all that for you:

    - Expose your data over an HTTP interface, unlike a database like PostreSQL
    - Use simple HTTP queries, instead of SQL
    - Use Kinto.js to easily implement offline first clients
    - Choose the database you want from those that Kinto supports, and use a unified API to access its data
    - Administrate your data using the handy admin UI
    - Easily set up live push notifications for live synchronisation
    - Make it possible to  share data between users (using fine-grained permissions)
    - Take advantage of schema validation if you need it
    - Use tools and libraries like Kinto.js that implements encryption so you don't have to
```

About the wording: we try in general in the documentation to avoid using "you".

Lastly, one of my tests was failing when checking for an error upon setting an un-registered key in a nested group. This is because the `Group.defaults` attribute is mutable since it only returns a shallow copy of defaults - this must be a deepcopy when there can be nested mutable objects. I agree that this is in conflict with the Thread spec.   I believe this is a spec issue that we need to raise with the Thread Group.

The specific scenario we are trying to handle by assigning a link-local RLOC is when a SED is sending IEEE 802.15.4 Data Requests to a parent that has no knowledge of the SED.  The only feedback a SED receives when sending Data Requests is the IEEE 802.15.4 Acknowledgement and a parent will happily acknowledge Data Requests even if it has no knowledge of the SED.  As a result, the parent must be able to send a Child Update Request to notify the SED to reattach.  Because the parent has no knowledge of the SED, the parent can only used information contained in the Data Request itself (e.g. a Source Address which may be a Short Address).

I guess you have some problem in you scrapy project,
something that makes it break in the environment of your scrapyd.

Scrapyd is also responsible that you didn't realize it.
`raise RuntimeError(msg.splitlines()[-1])` strips the whole traceback
and you can't know where/how the exception occurred.
This will be fixed on a later version.

If in scrapyd's source you replace the line
`raise RuntimeError(msg.splitlines()[-1])`
with `raise RuntimeError(msg)`
you will see the whole traceback
and find what's wrong in your project.

There is a memory leak. Here's a python3 program that demonstrates it (replace `gb = 16` with however much memory your computer has). 

```
import numpy as np
import swiglpk as glpk

def alloc1mb():
    'allocates 1MB memory that should be freed when the function exits'

    size = 1024 * 1024 // 8 # 1 MB

    my_list = [0.0] * size

    arr = glpk.as_doubleArray(my_list) # runs out of memory
    #arr = np.array(my_list) # works fine
    #arr = glpk.doubleArray(size) # also works fine

def main():
    'main func'

    gb = 16 # how many GB to go through

    for k in range(1024 * gb):
        if k % 256 == 0:
            print("{:.2f} GB so far".format(k / 1024))
            
        alloc1mb()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

If you watch the memory usage on your computer as it runs, you'll see memory quicky gets exhausted, whereas using `doubleArray` directly or `np.array` works fine (the memory gets freed after the function exits and can be reused).

Also, in the implementation of `as_doubleArray` and `as_intArray`, there's no checking if `malloc` fails, which it can.

So I've been looking into this issue for a while and I think the issue might actually lie with CloudFormation but probably for a good reason.

Take, for example, the sequence: `012345678`. The yaml spec (v1.1, it's fixed in v1.2) says that octal numbers start with a `0`. But this sequence isn't valid octal because it contains `8` so most yaml parsers interpret this as a string. CloudFormation does not and (possible incorrectly) tries to interpret it as a number. This is wrong from the point of view of the yaml spec but probably correct from a safety point of view.

Fixing this in cfn-flip is going to be interesting but I'll continue to look into it.

If you have any ideas, please submit a PR :D

The breakage is introduced on commit `f2457b3`. It looks to me like it gets into a situation where the _schedule_remaining_events() _handle_events() task scheduling breaks, and therefore there are no scheduled tasks to handle new events. The behavior is that a large number of messages are sent all at once and then no receive events are handled (because neither _schedule_remaining_events() or _handle_events() are scheduled tasks any longer). If I call self._add_io_state(self._WRITE) at the end of _add_send_event() regardless of whether self._send_futures has items, then things work as expected.

I modified the [asyncio/coroutines.py](https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/master/examples/asyncio/coroutines.py) example,
the ROUTER/DEALER sockets works well with tornado under pyzmq17.0.0/libzmq4.1.6/Tornado5.0.2


```python
import time

import zmq
import asyncio
from zmq.asyncio import Context,install
install()

from tornado.ioloop import PeriodicCallback


ctx = Context.instance()
url = 'tcp://127.0.0.1:5555'

async def ping():
    """print dots to indicate idleness"""
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        print('.')

from tornado import gen 

class Receiver(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pull = ctx.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
        pull.bind(url)
        self.sock = pull
        print("Router:", self.sock.fileno() )
     
    #!!! 此函数必须是 @gen.coroutine和yield，不能使用 async和await    
    @gen.coroutine
    def call_back(self, sock, events):
        print("recving", events)
        msg = yield sock.recv_multipart()
        print('recvd', msg) 
    ###此函数不能为async，原因未知        
    def run(self):
        poller = IOLoop.current()
        poller.add_handler(self.sock, self.call_back, zmq.POLLIN)


class Sender(object):
    def __init__(self):
        sock = ctx.socket(zmq.DEALER)
        sock.connect(url)
        self.sock = sock
        print("DEALER:", self.sock.fileno() )
        self.period_callback =None
        self.tic =  time.time()
    
    #@gen.coroutine  
    async def period_check(self):
        print("sending")
        await self.sock.send_multipart([str(time.time() - self.tic).encode('ascii')]) 
            
    def run(self):

        if not self.period_callback :
            self.period_callback = PeriodicCallback(self.period_check,callback_time=1000)
        if not self.period_callback.is_running():
            self.period_callback.start()


from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
loop = IOLoop.current()
loop.spawn_callback( ping)
recv = Receiver()
loop.spawn_callback( recv.run )

send = Sender()
loop.spawn_callback( send.run )
loop.start()
```

I think I tracked the problem down to this change in Python 3.2: http://hg.python.org/cpython/rev/c892b0321d23

I believe this is the approximate sequence of events:
1) A message is created at message.pyx:253 with the "free function" assigned to free_python_msg (I think on the IO thread)
2) Python interpreter goes into shutdown mode.
3) libzmq calls the free function associated with the message (i.e., free_python_msg).
4) Because of the Python change linked above, when free_python_msg tries to acquire the GIL, Python forcefully exits the thread.
5) The remaining cleanup libzmq needed to do on that thread never happens, eventually leading to the assertion.

I have no idea how to get started fixing this, but it definitely appears to be a Python/pyzmq issue and not a libzmq one. I suspect this "PyThread_exit_thread() if trying to acquire the GIL during shutdown" could cause all kinds of unclean shutdown issues with libzmq threads running.

@Lukasa: 12.04 is an LTS release just like 14.04 and 16.04 will be. Ubuntu supports LTS releases for at least 5 years, providing security and package updates. 12.04 is still a fine choice and is not EOL for another year and a half.

Regarding opt-in, I am not sure what time interval makes sense. I honestly don't know enough about the subject matter to say what is reasonable, since I am not a security researcher. That's why I use certifi: because I can't put together an equivalent thing on my own (and I appreciate the work that gets put into it). However, when a breaking change is released that brings production sites to a halt it is difficult justifying trusting it.

You mention that explicit failures get fixed faster. In this case that won't happen. Since the next LTS Ubuntu release is not due for another 7 months, the solution we went with was simply to pin certifi to the pervious version. It will likely stay this way until we are able to get openssl 1.0.2, some time in 2016. I suspect most people are doing this right now, or are using certifi.old_where(), both of which effectively negate the increased security the new version provides. Pinning the certifi version also will prevent any future updates going out which will mean that lots of codebases will become less secure, not more.

It just feels like there should be a better solution than dropping a huge breaking change over the weekend. Distros and browsers manage to stay on top managing root certs without suddenly breaking sites like amazon.com, paypal.com, ups.com, etc. Is there some type of process that they follow that allows them to provide such safety that certifi could mirror?

The challenge I see here is what the Python API would look like.

On the one hand, I'd really quite like it to be something a little bit like Django's [`get_or_create`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/models/querysets/#get-or-create) in the single-item case:
``` python
obj, created = (
    MyModel.objects
    .on_conflict(['name'], ConflictAction.UPDATE)
    .insert_and_get(name='swen')
)
```
However that would break existing usages of `insert_and_get` (and of `upsert_and_get`). Having another name for this would solve the problem, though I can't think of a good one right now.

While we could do something like attach a `did_conflict` value to the returned model, there's no way to do that for plain `insert`/`upsert` (which return primary keys). It's also highly non-obvious to users, so I'm rejecting this route.

The bulk scenario is a bit easier as there is no existing `bulk_insert_and_get` (though I have just noticed the `return_model` parameter to `bulk_insert` though that's only obeyed when a conflict is anticipated):
``` python
created_models, updated_models = (
    MyModel.objects
    .on_conflict(['name'], ConflictAction.UPDATE)
    .bulk_insert_and_get([
        MyModel(name='swen'),
        MyModel(name='henk'),
        MyModel(name='adela')
    ])
)
```

Hallo @dundeemt 

I think this looks really good. A few things crossed my mind while reading though it:

1) About the docstring. We have enabled napoleon for sphinx for our autogenerated API docs, which should pick up the arguments (with type) etc, provided that the doc string is formatted according to the google style doc strings. I can understand that you didn't notice, since we haven't really started the conversion of the docstrings yet, but that will happen. So if you don't mind, it would be nice if you could format new docstrings in this way. It should be something along the lines of:

```python
def to_dict(self, optimized=False):
    """Return a dictionary representation of the DidlResource

    Args:
        optimized (bool): Optionally filter dictionary to only with a value other than None.
    """
```

2) I'm unsure if optimize is the best (most descriptive name), maybe ``filter_out_nones`` or ``reduced``, I'll leave that entirely up to you
3) I completely agree with your evaluation about it being safe to filter out the Nones. For my part, the important part is that the dict can be used to re-instantiate the object by ``DidlResource(**resource_dict)``. In that respect, it should always be safe to filter out the default value, so I think that is a good idea.
4) Speaking of re-instantiating, would you mind also writing the symmetrical classmethod ``from_dict`` (just as the ``DidlObject`` has), as we usually prefer to have both there, so the user is not required to have any inside knowledge to be able to use these methods?
5) With regards to ``DidlObject.to_dict`` calling ``to_dict`` on ``DidlResource``. I would actually prefer that it did it and I think it is ok to break interface for. My reasoning for this is first and foremost that my main expectation of these methods should be that they are symmetrical, so provided that you modify ``from_dict`` on ``DidlObject`` too, that would still be the case. Second, I would also usually expect of a ``to_dict`` method, that it contains only simple types, so I would actually consider it more of a bug in the first place that a break of API. @lawrenceakka @stefankoegl do you guys have an opinion on the matter? If we do decide to change it, the interface change should be documented in the doc string.

I guess you are familiar with the github workflow, so if you do decide to make any change, you can just commit them to you the branch that you made the PR from (looks like your master branch) and they will show up here.

I apologize in advance for the verbose feedback, I don't really do short ;)

Thanks for you work on this.

I just remembered that you are probably using this for the Kodi plugin, in which case you may not see the exceptions. All the event parsing exceptions happen in their own threads, which mean that they do not make the program stop. > I think this commit is going to give subtle problems. IIRC Cython and setuptools incompatibly monkeypatch Extension, and using cython as a script is much more robust.

> Also, it looks like this requires Cython as a build-time dependency for released builds, which is not the case now. Or maybe that's fixed in a later commit (I'm reviewing commits one by one)?

No, but it should be straightforward to fix: http://docs.cython.org/src/reference/compilation.html#distributing-cython-modules

> And the hashing to prevent rebuilds as well?

Cython.cythonize does this internally. It also picks up if the installed version of Cython has changed and rebuilds (just noticed this now, neat).

```
./setup.py build
kai@laptop ~/D/c/pywt> ./setup.py build
Compiling pywt/_extensions/_pywt.pyx because it changed.
Compiling pywt/_extensions/_dwt.pyx because it changed.

[2/3] Cythonizing pywt/_extensions/_pywt.pyx
[3/3] Cythonizing pywt/_extensions/_swt.pyx
running build
running build_py
<LOTS MORE COMPILING>
kai@laptop ~/D/c/pywt> ./setup.py build
running build
running build_py
copying pywt/version.py -> build/lib.linux-x86_64-3.4/pywt
running build_ext
kai@laptop ~/D/c/pywt> 
```

> I don't quite see why you'd need the --where argument to nosetests? pywt is either on sys.path or it isn't I'd say.

If I'm in the working directory (next to `setup.py`), and I run `./setup.py build`, `nosetests` fails because the compiled extensions aren't in the working directory (and `build/lib-linux-whatever` isn't on `sys.path`). I need to run `./setup.py build_ext --inplace`, but it's a little irritating because it puts a bunch of `.so` files in my working directory. Though I guess Cython already does this with `.c` files so it's not a big deal. The above was an attempt to get nosetests to run in the `build/lib-linux-...` directory so the source tree stays as clean as possible.

I would like to have a categorical type too; of course it's up to @FrancescAlted and @esc , but I think in light of previous discussions, we can always add it to bquery (to keep bcolz focused + it's exactly the kind of thing that we're trying to add with bquery).
Of course, bquery needs some general love too at the moment (making it pip installable, parallelization, additional statistical methods, sort methods, etc.), but I think most of the building blocks for a categorical type are there.
Having read the pandas categorical doc, I'm a bit surprised at some of the functionality:
The fact that certain operations are only possible when the category is ordered (which most of the times you do not want to do, as often the column is part of a larger table)
How they are setting rows to NaN -> for me / bquery NaN atm technically is just another factorization value. 
I need to read more into r's factor too I guess :) 

For now it would mean:
- Add the categorical type (and maybe default to this for string columns?)
- Better add metadata to the carray (now we do this a bit "dirty" without writing proper metadata" and we now would need to start doing this in a more future proof way); also: atm bquery factorization is heavily oriented towards the disk-based version only
- Auto-factorize categorical columns while creating them or appending data to an existing column (which also needs checks as "is the dtype the same as the categorical carray")
- When adding statistical methods, check whether it's a categorical column and if so, handle it differently

Some reads that might be interesting for @ARF1 and @mrocklin to understand why we made bquery in the first place. The original discussion document based on which we started bquery: http://www.slideshare.net/cvaartjes/bcolz-groupby-discussion-document
And how my own company uses it: (I hope to open source this by the end of this year, main thing there is to disentangle propietary stuff with the general architecture):

keras.backend.set_learning_phase(0)

for i in range(2):

    for j in range(len(srcdata)):
        if i == 0:
            image = srcdata[j]
        else:
            image = advdata[j]
        label = srcLabel[j]
        x = np.expand_dims(image, axis=0)
        x = preprocess_input(x)
        preds = kmodel.predict(x)  #the code of predicting is used from the example code of [Keras](https://keras.io/applications/#mobilenet)

        if preds.argmax() == label:

Hi @isuruf I'm afraid that simplify expressions before sending to sympy can't fix #105. In the implementation of sympy's `cse` function, [line 530 of cse_main.py](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/c9af43024255c81541028b6accb400cb173b2c54/sympy/simplify/cse_main.py#L530), we can see that all expressions must be instances of sympy's `Basic` class. However, we can't inherit symengine's `Basic` class from sympy's `Basic` class, as cdef classes can't inherit from normal Python classes, see [cython doc](http://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/cdef_classes.html). So, just simplify the expressions can't fix #105.

I think the correct way to fix it is casting expression to sympy's value before sending to the `sympy.cse` function. I mean, something like this:

~~~python
    subs, new_exprs = cse([expr._sympy_() for expr in exprs])
~~~

Yea, users never import `_dynet` or `_gdynet`, and yea `dynet` init() with default global settings as part of importing process. I agree that it's natural not to require user to `init` by themselves, but probably not that ideal to use the current `dynet_config` to set globally:

> Suppose one has to import another module `dynet_config` BEFORE `dynet` to config –– which is the situation now –– it assumes that throughout the python program the global settings won't change. It's not bad, but for example it leads to a problem that when programmers wanna try dynet in jupyter notebook, they have to restart the whole kernel to reset the global params (because after `dynet` imported, `dynet_config` will not work; even if you try to import `dynet` again, `dynet_config` will still not work, due to the prevention of duplicate initialization).

post-init sounds nice.

Also, building on the previous GIF - suggestion in the other issue thread, using labels and a different coloring scheme, PHATE applied to the tutorial's example data could be slightly more visually compelling. It seems reasonable to me that a user (read, biologist) would want to see how the different time point's data splays along the PHATE axes. Converting the time points then from their matrix representation to explicit "Days" of extraction and using a color scheme that is diverging (perhaps pulled from here - http://colorbrewer2.org/) better depicts this progression.

```fig = plt.figure(figsize=(12, 12))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

ax.scatter(
    xs=Y_2Dmmds[:, 0], 
    ys=Y_2Dmmds[:, 1], 
    zs=Y_2Dmmds[:, 2],
    c=_y,
    s=1.28)

ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_zticks([])
ax.set_xlabel('PHATE 1', fontsize=16)
ax.set_ylabel('PHATE 2', fontsize=16)
ax.set_zlabel('PHATE 3', fontsize=16)

ax.text(-10, 0, -20, 'Day 0', color='#fc8d59', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(-5, 0, -20, 'Day 6', color='#fee08b', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(0, 0, -20, 'Day 12', color='#dae69c', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(5, 0, -20, 'Day 18', color='#99d594', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(10, 0, -20, 'Day 24', color='#3288bd', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold')

angles = np.linspace(0, 360, 129)[:-1]
rotanimate(ax, angles,'3d_scRNAseq_phate.gif', delay=8)```

according to the docs:

```
from annoy import AnnoyIndex
import random

f = 40
t = AnnoyIndex(f)  # Length of item vector that will be indexed
for i in xrange(1000):
    v = [random.gauss(0, 1) for z in xrange(f)]
    t.add_item(i, v)

t.build(10) # 10 trees
t.save('test.ann')
```

I tried and saw that when I have new items which I would like to include in the index, or to override existing items in the index, I can just use 
```t.add(i,v)``` for the new item, or item to be overridden, without adding all other items again.
then just using  ```t.build(10)``` to build the new tree.
The tree build step is quite fast, hence can fit for streaming use cases, in which I would like to update and rebuild the index in real-time for new streaming items.
Sometimes there are items which are deleted, and I dont want to recommend them, and here I see a big advantage to have the ability deleting those items.

一个与安全性无关的安全性问题：

现有的ss版本，为何用户的密码直接使用明文？虽然这段明文不会被直接发送只是作为加密的key（至少以我的理解是这样），但是直接导致存储时用户的密码也必须明文存储（客户端与服务端均是如此）。从软件设计的角度考虑这样的设计是否欠妥？
我的建议是：使用hash(password)作为加密的key，服务端与客户端的配置文件均只存储该哈希值，协议层仍然是不变的，只是加密所用的key多了一层哈希。

A security related issue without actual concerns about security:
In the current shadowsocks, password used for encryption key are stored in
plaintext (both client and server), though this password is not sent directly in
transmission. I don't think this complies with security standard. I would
recommend using the hash of password as an encryption key, e.g. md5(password).
And instead of storing the password in plaintext, store the hash in config. This
way we prevent any would-be attackers from getting the password plaintext. (And
service provider no longer require user password in plaintext, only the hash
need to be stored on server)

Hi, we're using event listeners often through PyMI, and here is an example:

[1] https://github.com/openstack/os-win/blob/master/os_win/utils/network/networkutils.py#L267

It's basically waiting for a given event_type (creation / deletion - __InstanceCreationEvent / __InstanceDeletionEvent) of Msvm_SyntheticEthernetPortSettingData objects (Hyper-V VM NICs).

From what I can see, PyMI only accepts raw WQLs for watch_for. From my experience, using a WQL query offers a lot more flexibility over what is returned by the watcher, as you can see here [2]. But, for simpler scenarios, we could build the WQL query based on the given arguments.

[2] https://github.com/openstack/os-win/blob/master/os_win/utils/compute/clusterutils.py#L88

Your code would work like this:

    import wmi
    c = wmi.WMI()
    wql = "SELECT * FROM __InstanceCreationEvent WITHIN 2 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_Process'"
    process_watcher = c.Win32_Process.watch_for(wql)
    for i in range(20):
        try:
            # milliseconds
            new_process = process_watcher(2000)
            print new_process.Caption
        except wmi.x_wmi_timed_out:
            pass

Best regards,

Claudiu Belu

Just execute Streamlink twice. You can do this in an interactive shell by simply executing two commands (`command1; command2; command2`) or by writing a shell script that translates parameters to multiple Streamlink commands.

Example in bash, which will run streamlink sequentially for each parameter (many input URLs):
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
for url in "${@}"; do
  file="${HOME}/downloads/$(date +"recording_%Y%m%d%H%M%S.mp4")"
  streamlink -o "${file}" "${url}" best
done
```

On Windows, you need to write your own script in powershell or batch or install bash.

In general, paragraph and font attributes for a given paragraph or run (respectively) are calculated by Word at run time by traversing the style hierarchy. This works a bit like Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) does for HTML. What you seem to be looking for is the *effective* paragraph spacing, which is the final result of this calculation/traversal.

The hierarchy starts out the way you've discovered so far, which is that directly applied formatting wins in every case, but an attribute is inherited from its style if not directly applied.

However, there are additional levels. A style can be "based on" another style, and inherits from that style any attributes it does not itself define. So that would be the next step. I believe there is a `.base_style` property available on both CharacterStyle and ParagraphStyle.

Then, I believe there is a document default style (one of each type, Paragraph, Character, etc.). It's been a while since I've had my head into this and it's not terrifically well documented on the web, so you may have to dig a bit. But as I remember, any attribute not defined by the explicit style graph is inherited from the document default style. I don't believe there is a property on style defined for this, but you could examine the style XML for the `w:default` property, maybe something like this:

```python
from docx.oxml.ns import qn  # short for "qualified name"

style_element = style.element
print(style_element.xml)
print(style_element.get(qn('w:default')))  # prints a boolean value or None if no such attr present
```

Finally, I believe there is a "built-in" set of defaults which rule in the case where no style of any type explicitly defines a setting. I believe these are hard-coded into Word, although themes may come into play for font and color choices.

So I expect if you dig a little deeper you'll find what you're looking for. I expect it will take a little experimentation to discover the exact rules Word uses, unless you have better luck than me with Google :)

Hi, @SwartzCr . I'm not asking you to release 1.0.0; I'm asking you to recognize that you've already released it. You're in production with tens of millions of users. def conv_block(x,num_convs,out_channels,block_id):
  for i in range(num_convs):
    x = mx.sym.Convolution(data=x,num_filter=out_channels,kernel=(3,3),stride=(1,1),pad=(1,1),no_bias=False,name='conv%d_%d '% (block_id,i+1))
    x = mx.sym.BatchNorm(data=x,name='bn%d_%d '% (block_id,i+1))
    x = mx.sym.Activation(data=x,act_type='relu',name='relu%d_%d' % (block_id,i+1))
  if block_id != 5:
    x = mx.sym.Pooling(data=x,kernel=(2,2),pool_type='max',stride=(2,2),name='pool%d '% block_id)
  return x

def fc_block(x,num_hidden,block_id):
  x = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=x,num_hidden=num_hidden,flatten=True,name='fc%d' % block_id)
  x = mx.sym.BatchNorm(data=x,name='bn%d' % block_id)
  x = mx.sym.Activation(data=x,act_type='relu',name='relu%d' % block_id)
  return x

def out_block(x,num_classes,block_id):
  x = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=x,num_hidden=num_classes,flatten=True,name='fc%d' % block_id)

def train():
  (x_train, y_train), (x_test, y_test) = cin.read_data()
  train_iter = mx.io.NDArrayIter(data=x_train,label=y_train,batch_size=100,shuffle=True,last_batch_handle='discard',data_name='input_data', label_name='input_label')
  valid_iter = mx.io.NDArrayIter(data=x_test,label=y_test,batch_size=100)
  data = mx.sym.Variable(name='input_data')
  softmax_label = mx.sym.Variable(name='input_label')
  conv1 = conv_block(data,2,64,1)
  conv2 = conv_block(conv1,2,128,2)
  conv3 = conv_block(conv2,3,256,3)
  conv4 = conv_block(conv3,3,512,4)
  conv5 = conv_block(conv4,3,512,5)
  fc6 = fc_block(conv5,4096,6)
  fc7 = fc_block(fc6,4096,7)
  fc8 = out_block(fc7,10,8)
  out = mx.sym.SoftmaxOutput(data=fc8,label=softmax_label)
  mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=out,label_names=('input_label',),data_names=('input_data',),context=mx.gpu(0))
  mod.bind(data_shapes=train_iter.provide_data,label_shapes=train_iter.provide_label)
  mod.fit(train_iter,eval_data=valid_iter,optimizer='sgd',optimizer_params={'learning_rate':0.1},initializer=mx.initializer.Uniform(0.1),eval_metric='acc',num_epoch=2)

Pinning dependencies in setup.py is not the right approach.
Pinning should happen in a requirements.txt

You can pin minimum-requirements in setup.py, 
but maximum-requirements only if you know for certain that new packages are not compatible AND you are not going to fix these issues. 
Always avoid exact pinning.

BTW major-version changes do not necessary imply breaking-compatibility, and minor-version  do not necessary imply the opposite. It should in theory, but in practice it doesn't.

Setup.py should just list its dependencies, 
it is not meant to create a reproducible deployment.

Packages listed in setup.py should never be pinned.
They do not serve the same purpose as a requirements.txt.

Pinning requirements in setup.py immediately creates a dependency-hell in larger projects,
which is the reason why am I searching this repo for a reason why this pinning happened.

> I'm not sure if I completely agree with the logic (or completely understand it from reading it). 
The only logic change is to make a Backfill run the number of dag runs that fit within the `max_dag_runs` limit, which I think it seems reasonable as it makes Backfill jobs consistent to what the limit is for.
>If we are able to run within max_dag_runs limits we should be able to run the whole backfill in one go
This is still the case, no change is done as `_process_backfill_task_instances` would receive all the tasks to run, same as the `while` loop implementation withing `_exectue` as before.
>, just a little bit slower: if a dag run finished a new slot becomes available, backfills wait till all runs finish so most likely they will get a slot except when the scheduler runs a new dag run. So what is the expected behavior? Do we wait till a slot becomes available (timeout needed?)? Do we expect the user to issue another backfill (might be confusing to the user).

All these are interesting questions, but I think they're out of scope of this PR - since these changes only try to enforce the `max_active_runs` limit correctly within a `BackfillJob`.

IMO any wait/retry logic should be made external to the `BackfillJob`, e.g. in the CLI running BackfillJobs for the same date interval or providing the user with `--tries` param or something alike. Including wait/retry logic within `BackfillJob` would only make it more complex and harder to maintain, test and debug.

Backfill is already a manual adhoc process run in the CLI, so as long as proper logging is provided to the user, they should be already aware of the `max_active_runs` limit, so providing some parameter which helps them decide how long to wait could be a good solution.

> In addition, as this is a huge PR, please stay in scope and don't fix details outside its scope (no changes to dagrun_exists, logging fixes etc). This makes it harder it rollback if required, besides you could introduce bugs that are unrelated.
>
> I prefer a PR that splits the refactor and the new functionality - again easier to review also considering the above remarks (and agreeing with @aoen) and easier to rollback if required.

Agreed, I've changed the branch to include two commits now:
- the first with no logic changes and just the refactor of the code within `BackfillJob._execute` to functions within the same class. Refactor is within scope as it only modifies parts of the previous `_execute` method and only touches `{airflow,tests}/jobs.py`
- the second with the change in logic to restrict dag runs in backfill job to the `max_active_runs` limit.
